text: AMUR, a vast river of Chinese Tartary, which rises in Mongolia in N lat. 48° 52', and 8 degrees W of the meridian of Pekin, or 108° 27' E of Greenwich; in Mount Kentey, or Kinhan, called Kingkan-Aleen by Klaproth. It is there called the Onon. After a course of more than 200 m. E and NE, it enters Russian Dauria, where, after receiving a multitude of mountain-streams from the NW side of the Kinhan, it receives the Ingoda, a stream nearly equal to itself, originating 330 m. to the SW, on the NE side of the snowy range of Suhonda, which gives birth on its NW side to the Shikoi, a tributary of the Selenga, and on the SW to a number of torrents descending to the Onon. The confluent stream runs NE to Nerchinsky, the capital of Russian Dauria, where it equals the Rhine in size, being 660 yards wide, and very deep. AMW Running still NE, after a farther course of 170 m., it receives, in N lat. 52° 40', the powerful stream of the Argun, which forms the Russian boundary S of the Amur, as far up as the Kulon-nur. The Argun may be denominated the twin-sister of the Amur, being its great southern branch, rising under the name of the Kerlon, on the S side of Mount Kentey, 7° 30' W of Pekin, in N lat. 48° 35', about 40 m. to the SE of the Onon Sekim, or 'source of the Onon.' After running in a direct course 500 m., and more than 600 by the windings, and watering the richest pastures in Mongolia, it enters the lake of Kulon, and passing through it, receives the name of the Argun, and joins the Amur after a farther course of 300 m., receiving a host of minor streams from the great range of Siolki, particularly the Kalka, which gives name to the Kalkas, a powerful Mongolian tribe. Thirty miles below this confluence, the united stream receives, on the N side, the Ajighe Kerbechi, or Gorbitza, from the Yablonnoi mountains, a river which forms on that side the Russian boundary. After a farther course of 80 m. almost due east, in N lat. 53° it changes its direction, running thence 630 m. SE, as far as N lat. 48°, where it receives the Sunggari from the SW, a deep and navigable stream. Thence it changes its course again to the NE, receiving the Usuri from the S, after a course of 320 m., and the Hata Hala, a stream of equal size, from the Hinkan Alin, with a multitude of minor streams on either side. Fifteen miles above the city of Saghalien-ula-Hotun, in N lat. 50° and E long. 128°, it receives the large stream of the Chikiri, called Zia by the Russians, which rises in N lat. 55°, in the Yablonnoi range. This stream receives a multitude of others, both from the Yablonnoi and the Hinkan Alin, and has a SW course of more than 400 m. It is 13 m. broad at its confluence with the Amur; and so rapid that it requires more than two months to ascend it, though it may be descended in fifteen days in a boat. The Amur, after receiving a multitude of other streams, finally enters a large gulf of the Pacific formed by its mouth, in N lat. 53° and E long. 142°, opposite the NW end of the island of Tchoka or Saghalien, by a channel 3 leagues wide, and very deep and rapid. The length of its course, including sinuosities, is estimated at 2,280 m.; and its average discharge of water, per second, at 298,800 cubical feet. Its basin contains a surface of 900,000 sq. m. It is navigable as far up as Nerchinsky, a distance of 1,500 m., for vessels of large burden. Its mouth is concealed by a vast number of aquatic plants; but the channel, deep and still, presents no impediments to navigation, having neither rocks nor shallows, and its banks are lined with magnificent forests.
AMURGUR, a fortress in the Punjab, on the road from Lahore to Kashmir, in N lat. 32° 55'.

AMURU, a district to the S of Abyssinia, skirting the Abai, between Limmu on the W, and Horro on the E. Dr. Beke was informed that the chief of this district has subjected most of the neighbouring tribes to his sway.

AMWELL (GREAT), a parish in Hertfordshire. Area 2,510 acres. Pop. 1,545. The name is supposed to be derived from Emma's well, a fountain which forms one of the sources of the New river. On an islet formed by the New river, stands a monument to the memory of Sir Hugh Middleton, who impoverished himself by the formation of the New river, to which London owes a large supply of water. This artificial river is 36 m. in length, having 43 sluices and 218 bridges. It was begun in 1606, and was completed in six years. In the church, the poet Warner, author of 'Albion's England,' lies interred. The village is 1½ m. SE by S of Ware.—Also a township in Washington co. Pennsylvania, U. S., on Ten mile creek. Pop. 1,536.—Also a township in Hunterdon co. New Jersey, on the S branch of the Raritan. Pop. 3,070.

AMYOT (Mount), the southern extremity of a long ridge in New South Wales, on the r. bank of the Lachlan, in S lat. 33° 20'. It consists of ferruginous sandstone. By the natives it is called Camerberdang.

AMYUN, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Tripoli, the chief place in the district of El Koura, 8 m. S by W of Tripoli. AN, or ANWEN, a village in Belgium, in the prov. and 7 m. ENE of Luxemburg.
ANA, or Eno, a town in Russia, in the gov. of Finland, near the SW extremity of Lake Pielis, 80 m. E of Kuopio, in N lat. 62° 54'.

ANA, or ANAH, a town on the SW frontiers of Asiatic Turkey, on the r. bank of the Euphrates; in N lat. 34° 20', E long. 41° 48', 160 m. NW of Bagdad. It occupies a narrow strip of land between 5 and 6 m. in length, stretching along the river, and skirted on the left by a chain of hills. Opposite to it, in the river, is a range of richly cultivated and picturesque islands; and beyond, a bare mountain-ridge rises abruptly from the river's brink. The town consists of one long, narrow, winding, but good street; and, for an Arab town, is remarkably clean. The houses, which are clay built, are about 1,800 in number, and are interspersed with numerous date-groves. The bazaars are poor, but afford common supplies. There are two mosques, and about 16 irrigating or wheatmills, the parapet walls of which extend from the aqueducts to the islands, leaving only a narrow passage for navigation on the left side of the river. The bed of the Euphrates at A., and to a distance, upwards, of 170 m., is extremely rocky; its ordinary depth is from 6 to 9 ft., but in many parts where rocks and shoals exist it does not exceed 22 inches, or 2 ft. Vessels, however, of even 14 or 15 ft. of beam are, by lightening more or less, enabled to navigate this part of the river at all times of the year. The ordinary ferry-boats at A. are flat-bottomed, and formed of inflated goats' skins. On the lowermost and principal of the islands, the ruins of the ancient Anatho commence, and extend about 2 m. in an easterly direction along the left bank of the river. The chief objects of interest belonging to the locality are the remains of four ancient castles—one of them on the great island —and a beautiful Arabesque minaret 80 ft. in height. The preparation of wool and cotton, the former in considerable quantities, constitutes one of the principal industrial occupations of the inhabitants of A. A. species of coarse cloth used for cloaks by the Arabs is its only manufactory. The inhabitants-almost exclusively Arabs—who, though addicted to plunder, are reputed much superior in character to the generality of their countrymen in the vicinity—belong to numerous small tribes, principally subject to the great one of Shamar. The defenceless condition of the town renders it liable to hostile attacks; one of the most aggravated description was perpetrated in 1807 by the Wahabees.—Parl. Report.
ANABARA, a river of Siberia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, rising under the Arctic circle, running north through the country of the Tunguses, and entering the sea in about N lat. 72°, E long. 108°.

ANACAPRI, a town of Naples, in the isle of Capri. It is finely situated on Mount Solaro, in a romantic situation, affording extensive and beautiful views to the NW of the Tyrrhenian sea, the gulf of Naples, Vesuvius, Misenum, &c. The town, which is ascended to by a flight of 552 steps, comprises a few streets, a church, and a convent, with a castle in the vicinity.
ANACHUANA, a village on a bay of the same name, on the coast of Colombia, between the point and the peak of Carreto. ANA
ANACLACHE, a majestic nevado or snowy peak in that chain of the Bolivian-Andes called the cordillera of the Coast. Its elevation is probably about 18,500 ft.
ANACOPIA, or ANAKOPIA, a small fortified town on the Black sea, in the country of the Abasians, 40 m. W of Isnagur. It has some inland trade, and a small but insecure harbour.
ANACRIA, a town on the Black sea, in Mingrelia, 52 m. W of Koutais.
ANACUR, or Nakour, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Syria, 6 m. N of St. Jean d'Acre.

ANADIA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, in the Sierra de Alcoba, 26 m. NE of Coimbra. -Also a v. of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco. ANADIR, ANADYR, or ANADIRSKAI, an extensive gulf or sea of Asia, at the NE extremity of Siberia, in the gov. of Irkutsk, opening between Cape St. Thaddee and Cape Tchukotskoi.—Also a river of Siberia, issuing from Lake Yoanko, in the Stanovoi mountains, about N lat. 67° 40', E long. 167° 10', and flowing into the gulf now described, in N lat. 64° 15', after a course of 500 m., through a country covered with rocky and barren hills, and buried under snow for nine months in the year. ANADO'LI, ANATO'LIA, or NATO'LIA, the largest and most westerly of the pashaliks of Asia Minor, comprising upwards of one-half of the entire extent of the peninsular portion of Asiatic Turkey, or Asia Minor. It is bounded on the N by the Black sea; on the NE by the pash. of Sivas, from which, to a considerable extent, it is separated by the Kizil Irmak; on the SE by the pashaliks of Caramania and Ichil; on the S and W by the Mediterranean; and on the NW by the Hellespont, the sea of Marmora, and the straits of Constantinople. It stretches from N lat. 36° to 42°, and from E long. 26° to 34° 40'; and probably contains, exclusive of the numerous islands which skirt its western coasts, a superficies of 100,000 sq. m. Its SE confines are skirted by the Sultan- Dagh, a branch of the Taurus chain, which enters the pash. at the western angle of the adjacent pash. of Karamania, and divides into several ramifications, one of which, running towards the S, terminates at Cape Khelidonia. One of the highest summits of this branch is Mount Takhtalu, the ancient Solyma, which rises near the western shore of the gulf of Adalia to the height of 7,800 ft. above sea-level. To the NW of Bazar khan, the summit of the Baba-dagh attains a great height, and gives rise to the Kengiz and the Umgi or Danis which fall into the Mediterranean to the W of the gulf of Macri. Another branch called the Kestane-dagh, the Messogis of the ancients, forms the northern boundary of the basin of the Mendere or Mæander. The Kisilja-musa-dagh, or Tmolus, detaches itself from the preceding range near the head of the Little Mæander, and running towards the archipelago, terminates at Cape Caraburun. Between this range and the river Kodus rises Mount Sipylus. Towards the western extremity of the series of mountainsinuosities which extends across this pash, towards the Hellespont, or in a general direction from E to W, is Mount Ida; and the western point of the crescent-shaped range which encloses the headstreams of the Gatipo, one of the principal tributaries of the Sakaria, is formed by the far-famed Mount Olympus. That range gives rise to the Mæander, and the Kodus or Hermas, both which rivers, after traversing nearly the entire breadth of the pash., fall into the Ægæan sea. The Grimalki, or Caicus, which, after a comparatively brief course, terminates in the gulf of Sandarli, also rises in this chain. The Ala-dagh separates the sources of the Olu and the Filbasu from the Upper basin of the Sakaria; and bending N along the western bank of the Filbasu, extends to Cape Guelimili, on the S shore of the Black sea. The Ulgaz-dagh, the most easterly of the mountain-ranges of A., terminates at Cape Indjeh. With the exception of the portion washed by the Black sea, in which the only great indentation is the gulf of Erekli, the coast of A. is remarkable for the sinuosities of its outline, forming numerous gulfs and capes, many of them of great extent, and studded with groups of islands of various dimensions. The principal headlands on the coast of the Black sea, which is 330 m. in length and is generally of considerable elevation, are Cape Indjeh, which forms the most northerly point of the peninsula of Asia Minor, in N lat. 42° 9', E. long. 34° 56'; Cape Kerempe, in E long. 33° 19', one of the highest capes on the Black sea; Cape Guelimili or Kilimoli; Cape Baba,—the eastern extremity of the bay of Erekli,—and to the W of the embouchure of the Sakaria, Cape Kirpeh. On the sea of Marmora are the gulf of Ismid, stretching from the southern extremity of the straits of Constantinople to Cape Bashurun; and to the S of this the gulf of Moudania. Passing the straits of the Dardanelles on the coast of the Archipelago, we enter the gulf of Adramyttium to the NE of the great island of Mytilene; and passing Cape Red, we enter the deep gulf of Smyrna, of which the western horn is formed by the peninsula of Cara-burun. The channel of Scio, between that island and the main, leads into the gulf of Scala Novo, another deep indentation of the western coast, whence the Little Boghaz leads between the island of Samos and the main, round Cape St. Mary into the gulf of Balat. Further S, Cape Arbova forms the northern horn of the gulf of Asyn Kale. The gulf of Kos, to the SE of the island of the same name which lies at its mouth, runs far up into the mainland. Cape Krio forms the southern horn of that gulf; and the gulf of Symi, which terminates at Cape Alepu, to the N of the island of Rhodes, completes the outline of the W coast. On the Mediterranean coast are the gulf of Makry, Phineka bay, and Cape Khelidonia, at the SE extremity of the obtuse promontory which forms the western side of the gulf of Adalia; and the western half of that large gulf into which the Aksu and the Dudon discharge themselves.—A. contains several lakes; but none of great extent. The principal are Lake Egerder, near the SE confines; Haji Ghiul, to the NW of the former; and Lakes Minias, Ulubad, and Iznik, near the shores of the sea of Marmora. The soil, consisting to a great extent of black clay, though much neglected, is generally fertile, admitting of the cultivation of the vine, the olive, and all the other vegetable productions common to these latitudes. The mountain-regions consist of calcareous rock, schist, trap, and chalk. In the vicinity of the Black sea they are generally well-wooded; but towards the interior, they often present entirely naked summits; and in some localities they exhibit a high state of volcanic action, their cavities frequently emitting volumes of flame and naphtha.—The climate in its general character is temperate and salubrious. Snow seldom falls; and even when it caps the higher summits, the vallies may be seen covered with luxuriant vegetation. The sea-breezes which prevail in summer moderate, in the districts exposed to their influence, the intensity of its heat; other districts lying towards the E are scorched by the sirocco; and all those portions of the surface which do not admit of irrigation become, during the drought which prevails -generally without intermission-in the months of June, July, and August, entirely arid. From the neglected state of agriculture, the produce of the soil is inadequate to the local consumption. Rice is Ottoman empire. Four-fifths of the inhabitants I.

ANADOLI. grown in considerable quantities on the banks of some of the rivers; but a large proportion of the supply is drawn from Egypt. Gardens are numerous, and produce excellent leguminous and pot herbs, and melons. The grapes, figs, and cherries of the orchards are of delicious quality. Wine of various kinds is made in considerable quantities; but it soon loses its flavour. The districts adjacent to the Black sea are covered with forests of oak, cyprus, and elm; and a portion of the Taurus chain, near the Mediterranean, is equally well-wooded. The shores of the Archipelago are adorned with the box-tree, the arbutus, and the mastic. Pasturage of the most luxuriant description is abundant; yet few sheep or cattle are reared in A. The wool of the sheep is of inferior quality, except in the environs of Angora, where it assumes the remarkable silkiness and length for which the hair of the cats and rabbits of that district is likewise distinguished. The horses are generally of handsome breeds; and the mules and asses are well-adapted for hard labour. Buffaloes are frequently employed for draught, and camels are occasionally to be met with. Stags, gazelles, deer, and hares are very common. Birds of passage, especially the quail, are numerous; and the rivers abound with fish. A. contains mines of copper, lead and iron; quarries of marble, lime, freestone, fine white clay, salt-petre and gypsum; several mineral springs and saline lakes and marshes. The chief articles of produce or of commercial importance are tobacco, hemp, lint, madder, indigo, poppies, saffron, sesame, silk and cotton. Honey and wax are produced in great abundance in the vicinity of the Black sea and of the sea of Marmora. Pitch is manufactured in considerable quantities in the environs of Sinope. Cheese is made in the vicinity of the sea of Marmora, but it is not of good quality. The principal manufactures of the pashalic are silk and cotton fabrics of various descriptions; the chalis or camlets of Angora; gold and silver tissues, bonnets, gloves, stockings, coverlets, sail-cloth, tobacco-pipes, and combs. Notwithstanding the exactions of its governors, the inhabitants of A. seem to enjoy an amount of personal comfort and liberty rarely to be met with in the Ottoman empire. They conduct an active commerce, consisting in wool of different kinds, camels' hair, the hair of the tiftik, a species of goat, silk, cotton, skins and tongues of buffaloes, hares' skins, wax, honey, cheese, butter, wine, rice, fruits, gall-nuts, soda, timber, turpentine, potter's clay, various articles of manufactural produce, &c. The ports on the Black sea supply Constantinople with salt-fish. The pash. or vice-royalty of Anatolia is divided into 17 sanjaks or military districts, viz., Kójah ílí, Bóli, Kastamuni, Kankari, Enkureh or Ammúriya, Kará-Hisár-sáhib, Hamid, Tekeh, Menteshá, Aïdín, Sighlah, Sárú-khan, Kará-sí, Bíghá, Khudávendikár, Sultán-Oní, and Germiyán or Kútáiya. Of these, 14 are governed by the pasha, who resides at Kutaiya Sighlah; and 3 on the coast, belonging to the pashalic of the Islands, are under the administration of the Kapúdán pasha of the empire. The district of the N coast, between the Kizil-Irmak and the Sakaria, is under a Turkoman chief, a successor of Cara-Osman-Oglu, who pays tribute to the Porte, but who rules with almost absolute sway. The principal towns and cities are Uskudar or Scutari, Izniknud or Nicomedia, Burusah or Prusa, Maghnisa or Magnesia, Alchisar or Thyatira, Izmir or Smyrna, Ayasoluk or Ephesus, Antaliyah or Attalia, Erekli or Heraclea, and Sinub or Sinope. The pash. of A. is said to be the most densely peopled in proportion to its extent of any portion of the are Turks; the remainder are chiefly Greeks and Armenians. Hordes of nomadic Turkomans, who are principally cultivators of the soil, locate themselves in winter in the interior. The languages in commonest use are the Turkish, Greek, and Armenian; and in the commercial towns French.—The geography of this district is further elucidated in the article ASIA MINOR.
ANADORN, a village in co. Down, 4 m. W of Downpatrick.

ANAFA, or DAR-EL-BEIDA, a small port of Marocco, in the district of Fez, in N lat. 33° 27', in a beautiful and fertile locality. It formerly belonged to the Portuguese, and was then very flourishing, but is now rapidly falling into decay. The neighbouring plain is very fertile in grain.
ANAFONITRA, a town in the island of Zante, near the NW coast, in N lat. 37° 52'.
ANAGADA. See ANEGADA.
ANAGALLIS, a town of South Africa, in the district of Georgia, on a branch of the Roka, in S lat. 33° 47′.

ANAGNI, a decayed town, the representative of the ancient Anagnia, in the States of the Church, and prov. of Frosinone, 38 m. SE of Rome. Pop. 5,450. It occupies a lofty and commanding position in the vicinity of Monte Cassino. It is the see of a bishopric. In the vicinity are the hot springs anciently called Therma Anina. A. was the birth-place of Pope Boniface VIII.
ANAGOU, a district of Upper Guinea tributary to Dahomey. It is about 165 m. from the sea; and is bounded on the NE by the kingdom of Dahomey; on the S by Kerrapay; and on the NW by Burrum.
ANAH. See ANA.
ANAHILT, a parish in co. Down, 3½ m. SE of Hillsborough. Area 6,777 acres, of which 465 consist of bogs and lakes. Pop. 3,551.
ANAHUAC. See MEXICO.

ANAJAZ, a river of Brazil,—the largest in the island of Maraho or Joannes,—which rises to the W of Salgado, and running in a westerly direction falls, after a course of 48 m., into the estuary of the Amazon. It is navigable at high water; and is much infested by alligators.

ANAKLIA, or ANAKRIA, a small but strongly fortified sea-port of Russia, in Mingrelia, at the confluence of the Enguri with the Black sea, in N lat. 42° 25'. The suburbs, which are extensive, consist of detached houses inhabited by Greeks, Jews, and Armenians. A. has a custom-house and carries on a considerable trade with Turkey. Pop. 600.
ANAKOSTIA. See POTOMAC.

ANAK-SUNGEI, a state in the island of Sumatra, extending along the SW coast, from the river Manjuta to the Urei. It contains mines of gold and copper, and produces excellent timber. Its capital is Moco-Moco, situated at the mouth of a small stream. This state has arisen on the ruins of that of Indrapura. Its first monarch, Gulema, was placed on the throne in 1695, by the assistance of the English. The government is Malayan; but the majority of the inhabitants are aborigines.
ANALABOO, a district on the NW coast of Sumatra, which produces considerable quantities of pepper.
ANAM, a small town of Hindostan, in the Oude territories, 34 m. SW of Lucknow.
ANAM. See Annam.
ANAMABOE. See ANNAMABOE.
ANAMARIS, a people of S America, who inhabit the banks of the Madeira, in the Brazilian prov. of Para. 120 m. above the confluence of that river with the Amazon.

ANAMBAS, three clusters of small islands and rocks in the Chinese sea, between the Malay penin- ANA sula and the island of Borneo. They are inhabited by Malays; and are called Scianthan by the natives. They are distinguished as the North or Little A., in N lat. 3° 27', E long. 106° 15'; the Middle or Great A., in N lat. 3° 2', E long. 105° 53'; and the South A., in N lat. 2° 18', E long. 106° 10'. The whole group extends about 150 m. from N to S, and as much from E to W. Their general structure is granitic.
ANAMICKEE, a co. in the NE part of Michigan, U.S., on the W side of Lake Huron. Area 600 sq. m.
ANAMIRAPUCU, a river of Brazil, prov. of Guayana, rising in the Serra de Araray, and, after receiving the tribute of several minor streams, flowing into the estuary of the Amazon.

ANAMUR, by the Turks called Eski-Anamur, a cape of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Itshil, opposite Cyprus, in N lat. 36°, E long. 32° 51'. This cape, the ancient Anamurium, on a coast generally bold, terminates in a high bluff knob, one side of which is inaccessible; the other has been fortified by a castle and outworks which are separated from the rest of the promontory by a flanked wall with towers descending to the shore. The promontory of A. is the most southerly point of Asia Minor; and affords, on its E side, good anchorage for coasters during a fresh breeze from the west. In the subjoined cut, from Captain Beaufort's work on Karamania, a a represents two lines of aqueducts, on different levels, which wind along the hill for several miles, and supply the fortress with water. The adjoining plain contains several villages, from which cattle may be readily obtained; and water may be had at the mouth of the Direk-Ondessi, supposed to be the ancient Arymagdus.—Beaufort.
ANANA, a town of Spain, in Biscay, prov. of Vittoria, 16 m. SSW of Orduna. There are salt-mines here.
ANANES, or Annanes, a cluster of three islets, in the Greek archipelago, about 11 m. SW of Milo.
ANANURI, a town and fort of Russia, gov. of Georgia, district of Seristo, on the river Arkala; 40 m. NW of Teflis, in N lat. 42° 5', E long. 44° 45'.
ANANY, a river of Brazil, prov. of Guayana, forming one of the branches which unite the Rio Negro with the Amazon.

ANAPA, a town of Circassia, in the country of the Abkhasses, on Sunjik bay, in the Black sea, 70 m. ESE of Theodosia, 46 m. from Kertche. This town, formerly 2 m. in circuit, is now a small hamlet, standing in a large plain in which the branches of the Caucasus terminate, and which stretches N and E towards the Kuban. It has a well-built khan, and is defended by a fortress, erected upon a rock about 100 ft. high, on the S side of the town. Towards the N the coast is low and marshy. Its pop. in 1824 was estimated by Marigny at 2,000. It exports hides, hare and sheep skins, tallow, wax, and honey; and the custom-house duties under the Porte amounted to from 15,000 to 40,000 piastres. Its roadsted is a little bay to the NE of the village. The anchorage is shallow and insecure. The site of A. originally belonged to a small Circassian tribe. In 1784, the Turks built A. to protect the Tartars who had taken refuge on the L bank of the Kuban, and the Nogois; and from that time, the productions of Circassia circulated chiefly through this new channel, as they had formerly done through Taman and Temruk, before these places were seized by Russia. In 1790, the Russian general Bibikow, with 8,000 men, was repulsed in an attempt to take A.; but in the following year, though defended by a Turkish garrison of 10,000 men, aided by 15,000 mountaineers, and a pop. of 5,000, it was taken by assault. Restored by Russia to Turkey, it was again taken in 1807, and the fortifications partly destroyed. The treaty of Bucharest in 1812 once more restored it to the Turks. In 1824 the sanjak of A. was united to the pashalik of Trebisond, and the Porte, to secure its possession, allowed considerable pensions to the leading Circassian chiefs in the vicinity. The Russians, who again took this place after a difficult siege in 1828, maintain themselves here with great difficulty. Convoys cannot come down the right bank of the Kuban without escorts of 1,000 men, although the distance between the fortress and the mouth of the river is not more than 30 m.-Marigny.-Spencer. ANA'PLI, NAUPLIA, or NA'POLI DI ROMANI'A, a town of the Morea, beautifully situated at the head of the gulf of Argos, upon the roots of a magnificent acropolis, which is crowned by a fortress so strong by nature that the Venetians made it their seat of government; as also did the Turks, until Tripolizza obtained a preference on account of its more central situation. [Hughes.] It stands on the NE slope of the hill, facing the main land. The houses rise up immediately from the water's edge. Its port is formed by the projection of a steep cliff across the NE side of the bay; but is now so much filled up with mud and rubbish as to be capable of admitting only small poluceas; yet previous to 1821 it was the depot of all the produce of Greece, and carried on an extensive commerce in sponges, silk, Velani acorns, oil, wax, and wines. The interior of the town is mean and filthy to an extreme [Pecchio]; but the view from the summit of Palamidhi, its acropolis, rising from it to the SE, is one of the most picturesque in the world. In the subjoined sketch A is the acropolis; B the situation of the flag-staff, or highest peak immediately to the S of the town; c is the landingplace; d the palace; e, entrance bridge; ff the line of aqueduct coming from a rocky ridge near Tiryns; G, Fort Bourgi, on a rock in the gulf. Dodwell, in 1805, estimated the pop. at 4,000; Waddington, in 1824, at from 7,000 to 8,000; but in the following year Count Pecchio thought it might amount to 15,000. The fortifications were chiefly built by the Venetians. They are very formidable, and if well-garrisoned, A. might be esteemed the Gibraltar of the Archipelago. Its climate is bad, and the town frequently suffers from epidemics, in consequence of its filthiness, and its vicinity to the large marsh which extends from the head of the gulf 2 or 3 m. inland. Its ancient name was Nauplia, written Nauplion by the modern Greeks. It is now generally called Anapli, corrupted Napoli and Di Romania ANC has been added to distinguish it from the Italian town of Napoli.—Pecchio.—Waddington.—Gell.—Leake.— Hughes.

ANARAJAPURA, the ancient cap. of the kingdom of Kandy, in Ceylon. Its ruins are about midway between the N extremity of the island and Kandy. Its circum. is said to have exceeded 60 m.; and nearly in the centre of the space formerly occupied by the royal city, is a "mean village," which still retains the name of A. A writer in the Naval and Military Magazine, [1835, Part 3,] who visited these ruins in 1834, describes them as gigantic and imposing. He saw, he says, 1,600 pillars, which, disposed in a square, supported one of the royal palaces; and four dagobahs, solid structures of brick, two of which were about 270 ft. in height; while the solid contents of one were 416,000 cubic yards! Lieut. De Butts says there are six dagobahs; and describes them as "immense solid domes, the alt. of which is equal to their greatest diam." They are surmounted by spiral cones, which, in some measure, relieve their vastness and massiveness.
ANA SANTA, the name of a number of small villages and settlements in South America.
ANASCO, a river of Porto Rico, forming a bay at its mouth, in N lat. 18° 30', W long. 67°.
ANASS, a river of Guzerat, rising in Malwa. Where it enters Guzerat its bed is as broad as that of the Dee at Bangor; but it appears to be left nearly dry during the hot season.—Heber.
ANASTAMIA, a port of Japan, on the S coast of Niphon. Its chief commerce is in wood.

ANASTASIA, or St. Anastasias, an isle of Florida, U. S., opposite St. Augustine, in N lat. 29° 50', W long. 81° 20'. It is about 25 m. in length, and is separated from the mainland by a creek called the Matanzas.
ANASTASIA (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Saint-Chaptes. Pop. 1,023.
ANASTASIO, a bay of Cephalonia, on the SE coast, between Capes Capro and Scala, in N lat. 38° 7'.
ANATOLIA (SANTA), a town in the States of the Church, prov. of Macerata, 6 m. S of Fabriano.
ANATOLIA. See ANADOLI.

ANATOLICO', a town of Western Greece, in Ætolia, at the entrance of a bay bearing the same name, 7 m. W. of Missolonghi. It is situated on a low island; and the water of the bay, or lagune rather, is so shallow on the W side that a mole projecting from the mainland reaches half-across to the island. A. is a point of great natural strength.
ANAVA, a river of Brazil, prov. of Guayana, rising in the Serra de Araray, and running eastwards to the Rio Branco, a tributary of the Amazon, into which it flows in 0° 49' N lat.
ANAVILHANA, or Anauillana, a river of Brazil, prov. of Guayana, running into the Rio Negro, about 50 m. above Fortaleza de Barra, opposite the islands known as the Anavilhanas.

ANAZO, a river of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Angote, formed by the united streams of the Ancona and Meli. After running nearly due E it takes the name of Yasso, under which appellation it falls into the sea of Bab-el-Mandeb, S of Ras-Bir.

ANBA'BO, a village on the shore of the bay called Ghubbat-el-Kharáb, on the Danakil coast of the Red sea, 4 m. WSW from Tajurrah. The road from the latter town to A. runs either on or parallel to the beach.
ANBAR, a town of Asiatic Turkey, prov. of Irak- Arabi, on the Euphrates, 45 m. W of Bagdad. - Also a town of Turkestan, prov. of Balkh, 70 m. SW of Balkh.
ANCA (Point), a headland of Chili, at the entrance of the Valdivia.
ANCAON, a town of Portugal, in Beira, 5 m. SE of Coimbra, near the confluence of the Deuca and Ceira. Pop. 400.

ANCASTER, a parish in the parts of Kesteven, Lincolnshire. Area 2,800 acres. Pop. 530. The town of A., which is 6 m. NE of Grantham, by many antiquaries is regarded as the Causennæ of Antoninus; and the Corinensis of the Itinerary of Richard of Cirencester.
ANCAYE, a district of Madagascar, on the E coast, not far from Foul point. The natives are called Bezounzons.
ANCE, or ANSE A' LA BARQUE, a bay on the S coast of Hayti, between Morne Rouge and Cape Baienet.-Also a bay of Lower Canada, on the r. bank of the Saguenay, about 2 m. from its mouth.
ANCE A' CATHARINE, a bay of Lower Canada, on the Saguenay, district of Quebec, formed by Point Allouettes, and the point of the Saguenay. It is about 2 m. wide, and from 2 to 30 fathoms deep.
ANCE DE BOTEILLE (CAPE A L'), a cape on the shore of Lake Superior, NW from Peek island. .
ANCE DE L'ETANG, a creek on the S shore of the estuary of the St. Lawrence, 16 m. NW 1 N from Great Fox river. A river issuing from several lakes falls into the head of it.
ANCE DES MORTS, a bay of Lower Canada, co. of Rimouski, on the Mitis river. It is a deep cove about 4 m. in circuit.

ANCE (GRANDE), a bay of Martinique, on the N coast between Point du Bourg and the Rocher Bourjeau, with a small river of the same name flowing into it, on which stands the town of St. Hyacinthe. - Also a large bay in the isle of San Cristoval, in Solomon's archipelago.—Also an extensive bay of Guadaloupe, in the district of Basse Terre, of which the Gros Morne headland forms the southern side.
ANCELLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, cant. of Saint-Bonnet. Pop. 1,104.
ANCE (Petite), a town of Hayti, on the N coast, on a bay of the same name, 5 m. SE of Cape Henry.

ANCENIS, a town of France, in the dep. of the Lower Loire, the chief place of an arrond., 21 m. NNE of Nantes, on the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. in 1846, 3,746. The Loire is navigable for small vessels to this town, which carries on a considerable commerce in wine, vinegar, beet-root sugar, corn, and wood.—The arrond. of A. is divided into 5 cantons, viz., Ancenis, Ligné, Saint-Mars-la-Jaille, Riaillé, and Varades. Its area is 78,217 hectares. Pop. in 1846, 47,397; in 1836, 45,765. The cant., which comprises 7 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 13,087.

ANCERVILLE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, arrond. of Bar-le-Duc. The cant., which comprises 18 com., had a pop. of 11,811 in 1831.—The com., which is 12 m. SW of Bar-le- Duc, had a pop. of 2,239 in 1841.
ANCHESMUS. See ATHENS.
ANCHESTRI, or ANGISTRI, an island off the coast of Argolis, in the Morea.
ANCHITTY, or ANCHITTYDURGAM, a fort of Hindostan, prov. of Coimbatoor, 25 m. from Coveriporam. It was taken by the British in 1799.

ANCHOLME, a river rising near Spridlington, Lincolnshire, and falling into the Humber near Ferraby, a little to the W of Barton. It has been made navigable from Glandford bridge, near Market-Raisen. Its total course does not exceed 30 m.
ANCHOR BAY, an anchorage in St. Estevan channel, on the W coast of Patagonia, of which the N point of entrance is in S lat. 50° 55', W long. 74° 17'.

ANCHOR ISLAND, near the southern entrance of Dusky bay, New Zealand, in N lat. 45° 46', E long. 166° 16'. On the northern side of this island is a secure harbour; but its narrow limits, and a sunken l ANC rock at the western entrance, render it inconvenient to get out to sea from.

ANCHOR ISLES, or Ancoras, two islets off the E coast of Brazil, 7 leagues S by W from St. Anne's isles. The easternmost is in S lat. 22° 46', W long. 41° 46'.— There are some islets bearing the same name in the Bissagos archipelago, off the SE end of Jatt.

ANCHOR POINT, a point of land on the NW coast of America, in N lat. 59° 39'.—Also a headland on the N coast of Van Diemen's Land, on the W shore of Port Dalrymple, where the width of the port is contracted to \frac{1}{4} m.

ANCHORITES, or HERMITS' ISLANDS, a group of islets in the Pacific ocean, to the north of Papua, first discovered by Bougainville in 1768, and visited by D'Entrecasteaux in 1792. The principal island is in S lat. 1°, E long. 145° 24'. This group is scarcely 14 leagues in circumference. The productions are similar to those of the Admiralty group.

ANCHUELA, a village of Spain, in New Castile, between Alcolea de Pinar and Used in Aragon. It is beautifully situated in a valley shut in by swelling hills, and the vicinity is fertile in corn, wine, and oil. -Townsend.
ANCIAONS, a town of Portugal, prov. of Tras-os- Montes, 12 m. W of Moncorvo. Pop. 1,200.
ANCIZAN, a town of France, in the dep. of Hautes- Pyrénées, in the cant. of Arreau, 15 m. SSE of Bagnères de Bigorre. Pop. 987.

ANCLIFFE, a hamlet in the parish of Wigan, in Lancashire. There is a celebrated well here called the Burning well, from the surface of which a vapour rises which takes fire on the application of a light, and burns for some hours if not disturbed.
ANCLOTES KAYS, a cluster of small isles, on the W coast of Florida, near St. Clement's point.
ANCO, a town of Peru, prov. of Heramango, on the Pampas, 48 m. above its junction with the Apurimac.

ANCOBAR, or Ancobra, or Gold River, a river of Africa, on the Gold coast, flowing into the gulf of Guinea, between Cape Apollonia and the Dutch fort of St. Anthony, in about N lat. 4° 48'. Its estuary is obstructed by rocks; but it has, in most places, about 12 ft. of water.
ANCOCUS CREEK, a river of New Jersey, U.S., running into the Delaware, 6 m. SW of Burlington. It is navigable 16 m.
ANCOLAN, a small isle off the N coast of the peninsula of Lucon in the Philippines, near the entrance of San Miguel's bay.
ANCOMANI, a peak in the Bolivian Andes, in the Nevado di Sorata. It probably attains an alt. of 24,000 ft.

ANCO'NA, a delegation or province of the Papal States; bounded on the N and W by the delegation of Urbino; on the E by the Adriatic; and on the S by the delegation of Macerata. It contains a superficial area of 75 sq. leagues. It is intersected by a chain of the Apennines, and its entire surface is bristled with mountain-ridges. Its vallies, watered by the Esino, Musone, Aspido, and Missa, are fertile, and produce wheat, Indian corn, lentiles, beans, hemp, wine, fruits, tobacco, and silk. According to a recent cadastral return, 1,104,000 lbs. of tobacco were grown in this province. The inhabitants are, generally speaking, industrious in their habits. They rear great numbers of cattle, sheep, and pigs; and manufactories of leather and silk, and various articles of taste, exist within the delegation. The principal exports are wool, corn, hemp, and silk. The climate of Ancona is pure and salubrious. Its pop. is estimated at 160,000. Ancona, the capital of the delegation, is situated on the Adriatic, in N lat. 43° 38', and E long. 13° 35', 12 m. NNE of Macerata, and 142 m. NNE of Rome. It derives its name from the form of the bluff headland, the Cumerium promontorium, on the side of which it occupies a strong position between two projecting heights, the Monte Ciriaco and Monte Comero or Guasco. It is the principal naval station in the Papal States; and is defended by a citadel, the only remaining portion of the ancient fortifications, and a fort. The harbour, naturally good, is protected by two moles, on the more ancient of which is the celebrated triumphal arch of Trajan, formerly the entrance to the old harbour, but now, by subsequent alterations, elevated above the quay. A similar monument in honour of Pope Benedict XIV., decorates the new mole; at the extremity of which is the lighthouse, constructed by Pope Clement XII. Although the depth of the harbour is considerable, it is liable to obstructions. Besides the cathedral-a singular structure of the 10th cent., which occupies the site of the ancient temple of Venus on the summit of the height on which the city is situated -A. contains a town-hall, government-house, arsenal, exchange, 10 churches, a college, 2 hospitals, 16 convents, and a lazzaretto. Its streets, with one exception, are narrow, ill-built, and gloomy; houses of the meanest description mingle with the large and wellfinished dwellings of the wealthy portion of the inhabitants. A. is the most commercial city on the E coast of Italy; and is the principal entrepot between Europe and the Levant, the Ionian islands, and Greece. With Corfu regular communication is established by steam-boats. In 1732 it was rendered a free port by Clement XII. The number of vessels which it annually receives amounts to upwards of 700. In 1841, 42 British vessels, with cargoes of a total value of £201,000, entered this port; and 64 left it with cargoes valued at £157,300. In 1840 the amount of British imports was £137,660; of exports £87,160; the total imports in the same year were £574,880; exports, £298,720. [Parl. Paper.] The chief articles of export to England are raw silk, wool, and creamof-tartar. Its chief trade consists in grain, wool, skins, silk, sail-cloth, sea-biscuit, soap, alum, verdigris, cream-of-tartar, sulphur, and fruits. An annual fair is held here on the 20th of August. The exchange of A. connects it with all the principal cities in Europe. It contains, with its suburbs, a pop. of about 36,000, of whom 5,000 are Jews, who reside in a particular district of the city. It is the seat of a civil tribunal, of a court-of-appeal for the marches, and of a bishopric; and is governed by a delegate, who is a prelate of the Roman church,—A. is said by Strabo and Pliny to have been founded about the year 400 B.C., by a colony of Syracusans. It was occupied by the Romans as a naval station; and was successively conquered by the Goths, Lombards, and Saracens; but eventually it erected itself into a republic. Having gradnually lost its independence, it was incorporated by Pope Benedict XIV., in 1532, with the Papal States. In 1797 it was seized by the French. In 1799 it underwent a siege by the Russians, Austrians, and Turks; and was captured after a gallant defence. Under Napoleon it was the cap. of the dep. of Metaurus; but was finally given to the pope by the congress of Vienna. On the 22d of February, 1832, the town and citadel were taken possession of by the French, and held by them, with the acquiescence of the Pope, until 1838, as a balance to the aggressions of Austria in the N of Italy.
ANCORA, an isle of Brazil, prov. of Rio Janeiro, 24 m. N of Cape Frio.
ANCORAYMES, a town in Upper Peru, at the foot of the Cordillera d'Acayma, on the eastern shore of Lake Titicaca, 70 m. NNW of La Pas.
ANCOVALLA, a port on the NW coast of Madagascar, in S lat. 13°.

ANCOVE, a district of Madagascar, nearly in the AND centre of the island inhabited by the people called Hovas and Amboilambs. It contains the capital of the island Tananarivo or Antana-Rivo; and is subdivided into the 3 districts of Vonizongo, Isamano, and Eurezina.
ANCRAM, a township of New York, U. S., on Ancram creek, 20 m. SE of Hudson. Pop. 1,770. Here are a lead-mine and extensive iron-works.

ANCRUM, a parish and village on the Ale river, in Roxburghshire, 31 m. NNW of Jedburgh. Pop. 1,407, of whom 499 were in the v. The soil is rich and well-cultivated; several excellent quarries of freestone are wrought here.
ANCTOVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Colvados, cant. of Caumont. Pop. 1,112.

ANCUD (EL), a large bay or gulf at the southern extremity of Chili, on the western coast of Patagonia. It contains a cluster of islands to which the Spaniards attached the dignity of a province, sometimes called Aqualay, sometimes El Ancud, and, in modern times Chiloe, from the name of the principal island. See CHILDE.
ANCY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Tarare. Pop. 988.
ANCY-LE-FRANC, a canton and town of France, dep. of Yonne, on the Armançon, 10 m. SE of Tonnerre. Pop. 1,423.
ANCY-SUR-MOSELLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Gorze. Pop. 1,104.
ANDA, a river of Russia, rising a little beyond Lake Angozero, separating the governments of Olonetz and Archangel, and flowing into the Vig.
ANDACOLLO, a town of Chili, prov. of Coquimbo, the seat of a mining district.
ANDAHUAYLAS, a town of Peru, prov. of Heramango, in a territory of the same name, between the Pampas and Pachachaca rivers, 55 m. SE of Heramango.
ANDAGIRI, or Andigri, a populous town of Sumatra, in a district of the same name, on a river said to be well-adapted for commerce. The chief production of the district is pepper.

ANDALGALA (LAGO DE), a lake of La Plata, prov. of Catamarca, about 50 m. in length from NE to SW, and 20 m. across in its widest part. It contains three islands; and receives, at its NE extremity, the superfluous waters of the Lago de Palcipa, through the Andalgala river. It also receives the Betlen river; yet has no visible outlet.

ANDALUSI'A, or more properly AndaLuci'a, the largest and most southernly of the provinces of Spain; situated between 36° and 38° 30' N lat., and 2° and 7° 20' W long.; and bounded on the N by Estremadura and La Mancha; on the E by Murcia; on the S and SE by the Mediterranean; and on the W by Portugal. Its length from E to W is 348 m.; and its average breadth from N to S is about 100 m. Its superficial extent is about 28,800 sq. m. - The southern districts of the prov. are intersected by the lofty ridges of the Pono-Betic range, which extends from Cape Tarifa on the W, to Cape Gata on the E, a distance of about 240 m. The highest portion of this chain, called from its snow-clad summits the Sierra Nevada, comprises the Cerro de Mulhacen, in N lat. 37° 6', W long. 3° 10', the loftiest mountain in Spain, which rises to the height of 11,660 ft. [Rozas Clemente] above sea-level, and the Picacho de Veleta, in N lat. 37° 4', W long. 3° 14', which has an alt. of 11,385 ft. [Ibid.] To the S of this range, and running in a parallel direction, are the Alpujarras which branch into the Sierra de Gador and the Sierra Contraviesto. The Sierra Nevada is connected by the Sierra de Cazorla with the Sierra Morena—the Montes Marianos of the Romans-a portion of the Marianique range, running from NE to SW, and forming the northern boundary of the prov. These mountainchains are intersected in all directions by vallies, and enclose many rich and beautiful level tracts, of which the finest and most extensive is the Vega de Grenada, an inclined plain about 90 m. in circumference, shut in on the N by steep mountain-acclivities, and in all other directions by hills rising in successive ranges, and intersected by five rivers and several canals. The vale of the Guadalquivir, the vallies of the Sierra Morena, and the country behind Cadiz, are perhaps the richest districts in Spain.—The principal rivers in A. are the Guadalquivir, which rises in the Sierra de Cazorla, and after traversing the entire extent of the prov., falls, with the waters of 28 subsidiaries, into the sea at San Lucar de Barrameda, after a course of 280 m.; and the Guadiana, which has a total course of 460 m., and for some distance forms the boundary line between Spain and Portugal. The Guadalquivir is no longer navigable to Seville for vessels drawing more than 9 ft. water.—A. possesses a great variety of climate. The temperature of the lofty ranges of Granada differs considerably from that of the mountains of Cordova, Jaen, or Seville. In the central districts, the heat of the plains is temperated by the mountain-breezes; but towards the coast, where it is exposed to the action of the African solano, it is often intense. Generally, however, the province is not insalubrious. Haussmann asserts that in travelling in Spain from N to S, no sooner is the Sierra Morena crossed, than the country assumes a novel and meridional aspect: an African tint is diffused, not only over vegetation, but over man and animals, by the exposure on the S and SW to the African winds, and the strong reflection of the solar rays from the steep and naked mountains. M. Huot comprises A. in what he calls the Bætic region of Spain, extending from Cape Palos on the E, to Cape St. Vincent on the W; and from the southern slope of the Sierra Morena and of the mountains of Algarve on the N, to the shores of the Mediterranean on the S. The tract skirting the sea in this region is African in the character of its vegetation, being characterised by the banana, the dwarfpalm, and the cactus. The zone N of this, and rising above it, embraces the flora of Italy and Spain. There the cystus, the myrtle, the orange, the lemon, the Agnus castus, and the tamarisk, bloom in rich profusion. To this zone succeeds that in which the staple vegetable productions of Europe thrive best. Beyond that is the region of the pine-forests; above which that of the Alpine plants stretches to the line of perpetual snow. Notwithstanding the number of rivers, irrigation is, in many districts of A., necessary to the productiveness of the soil; but its returns are of the richest description, consisting of large quantities of grain and fruits of all kinds and of surpassing quality. The sugar-cane is cultivated along the coast. The vine and olive grow luxuriantly in all parts of the prov.; the latter, in some parts, covers immense tracts; but the country being parcelled out in immense estates, pasturage is extensively followed, little land is enclosed, agriculture is in a backward state, and a considerable importation of corn is annually required for the wants of the pop. The forests of the Sierra Nevada and of the Sierra Morena afford many valuable articles of commerce. The principal production in the animal kingdom is the horse, of which the Andalusian breed is greatly celebrated [Buffon]—although it has very little in common with the English blood-horse. He is round in his quarters, broad-chested, long-legged, and long in the pastern [Ford].—The strong bull peculiar to this prov. is reared on the Sierra Morena. Sheep and pigs are abundant; wolves and boars are numerous in the forests, and monkeys on the rock of Gibraltar, and on the Sierra de Ronda. The scorpion, several species of snakes, the tarantula, and the chameleon are common. A. possesses mines of gold, silver, lead, copper, iron, quicksilver, arsenic, antimony, coal, &c., all of which—with the exception of the lead mines of Adra—have long been neglected. It contains likewise extensive quarries of the finest marble. The Sierra de Gador, which rises 2,600 veras above the sea, is one enormous block of marble; and the Sierra de Filabris, between Almeria, Granada, and Guadin, consists of a single ridge or bank of pure white marble, 2,000 ft. high. Alabaster and many species of precious stones are found in the mountain-districts, The commerce of Andalusia has suffered much from the depressing influence of the custom's laws and duties which now prevail in all the Spanish port, and which, to a great extent, have excluded the legal trader from competition with the contraband. It chiefly consists in the importation of colonial produce from Cuba, Puerto Rico, and the Philippine isles, in Spanish vessels. Under the same flag are imported into Cadiz, cocoa, hides, cochineal, indigo, and the productions of South America. Manufactured goods, such as silks, linen and woollen cloths, iron hoops, tin, glass, hard and earthen ware, butter, and cheese, are imported from England, France, and Germany in Spanish vessels. Cod-fish-still an article of considerable consumption in this prov.—is chiefly imported under the British flag. Large quantities of staves for wine casks, and occasionally some tobacco for the royal monopoly, are imported in American and other foreign vessels from the United States. Timber is brought from Russia, Sweden, and Norway.—The principal exports are wines, the finest of which are grown in the SW corner of the prov., in the immediate vicinity of Xerez. Fruits, olive-oil, cork, salt, wool, some brandy, anchovies, quicksilver, and the lead of Granada are also exported.—The chief manufactures exist at Cadiz and Seville; and consist of a coarse species of linen for home use, woollen cloths, hats, leather, tobacco, soap, glass, and earthenware.—Cadiz and Malaga are the only sea-ports of any commercial importance, though there are many minor harbours which carry on trade with the fisheries and the adjacent coasts. Cadiz is the most important sea-port in Spain. It possesses a good harbour; but its navigation is intricate from sandbanks off its entrance. Its proximity to the Atlantic renders it peculiarly available for the American trade. Malaga has a bar-harbour, but it is safe and commodious; and the town pursues an active trade in wine and raisins, and is the staple place for the commerce of the whole province. The annual value of its exports is estimated at £375,000. St. Lucar, at the mouth of the Guadalquivir, is of dangerous access, but is much frequented as being the harbour of Seville and Cordova. Within the bay of Gibraltar, Spain possesses a good port sheltered by a mole at Algeciras. The harbour of Almeria is well-sheltered and capacious. On the SW of the prov. is the Punta de Europa, on which stands the fortress of Gibraltar. Besides the sea-port towns already mentioned, this prov. contains Cordova formerly a flourishing city, now possessing a pop. variously estimated from 30,000 to 50,000; Seville, situated on the beautiful Guadalquivir, which connects it with the Mediterranean; and in its immediate neighbourhood Xeres de la Frontera, famous for its wine; Jaen, with a pop. of 19,000, or, according to other authorities, 27,000; Andjar, with 14,088, celebrated for its manufacture of a particular species of earthenware; and the beautiful Moorish city of Granada, with the magnificent palace of the Alhambra, and a pop. estimated at 80,000 The total pop. of the prov. was estimated, in 1826, at 1,543,000; of whom 608,000 belonged to the towns, and 935,000 to the rural districts. In 1787 it was returned at 788,153. Bory de St. Vincent estimated the number of monks in 1823 at 18,063, and that of other religious at 5,624. The Andalusians retain many of the characteristics of their Arab blood. They are a pleasure-loving, work-abhorring race, more celebrated for their social and intellectual qualities than for those of a practical and industrial character [Ford]. They speak the dialect of Castile, corrupted, however, by the admixture of Arabic words, and still more by false pronunciation. By a decree of the Cortes in 1822, A. was divided into the 8 provinces of Almeria, Cadiz, Cordova, Granada, Huelva, Jaen, Malaga, and Seville. This arrangement was annulled by a royal decree of 3d August, 1837, which attached the prov. of Granada, Almeria, and Malaga, to the captaingeneralship of Granada; and assigned to that of A. the provinces of Seville, Huelva, Cadiz, Cordova, and Jaen. Andalusia is supposed to have derived its original population from Mauritania. The mineral and vegetable wealth of the province soon attracted the cupidity of colonists from Tyre and Carthage, whose Tartessus this country was, and as early as the 3d cent. it became a portion of the Roman empire. In the Gothic invasion, this prov. was overrun by the Vandals, and hence it became known to the Moors—in common with a strip of the African coast on the opposite side of the straits—as the Belad-al-Andalosh, i. e., 'the Territory of the Vandal,' - whence its present name. At the beginning of the 8th cent. a signal victory was obtained by the Moors over the Goths in the battle of Xeres de la Frontera, and from that time A. became the chosen seat of the Moors in Europe. Under the Moors, Andalusia was divided into the four kingdoms of Seville, Cordova, Granada, and Jaen, which are sometimes known as the Andalusias.—Malte Brun.—Murray's Encyclopædia of Geog.—Laborde, Itineraire descriptif de l'Espagne, Tom II.—Townsend's Travels, vol. II.—Ford's Hand-Book and Gatherings. - Bory de St. Vincent, Guide du Voy. en Espagne, 1823.

ANDAMAN ISLANDS, a group in the bay of Bengal, forming the larger and more northerly portion of the island-chain which stretches from the southern extremity of the Birman empire to the NW of Sumatra. The Andaman cluster is situated between N lat. 11° 8' and 14° 50', and E long. 92° 21' and 94° 12'; and consists of two larger and several smaller islands. Of the former, the principal, named the Great Andaman, consists of three nearly equal parts, formed by the intersection of Stuart's sound and Andaman's straits, presenting a total length of 150 m., and in breadth varying from 18 to 20 m. Its coasts are indented by deep bays, forming excellent harbours; and the surface generally is of a hilly character. Its highest elevation, named Saddle peak, towards its NW extremity, rises 2,400 ft. above sea-level. It possesses no rivers of importance; but is watered by numerous streams, which, during the SW monsoon—which blows eight months of the year—swell into torrents. The surface is to a great extent covered with wood.—The Little Andaman, the most southerly of the Andaman group, situated about 30 m. to the S of the Great Andaman, from which it is separated by Duncan's passage, and within 90 m. of the most northernly of the Nicobar group, is 28 m. in length, and 17 in breadth. It is more elevated than the larger island; and equally well-wooded. Its coasts contain no harbours, but afford excellent moorage.—The minor islands are Preparis the most northerly of the group, Great Cocos, Narcondam, Barren, a volcanic island which rises 1,800 ft. above sea-level, and Rutland, to the S of and separated from the Great Andaman by Macpherson's strait. The action of the tides is regular; their direction is westerly, and their highest rise 8 ft. The variation of the needle is 2° 30' easterly. The principal produce of this island-group is timber, consisting of teak, iron-wood, red wood and ebony. The mangrove affords one of the principal articles of subsistence to the natives. Melons, ground-rattans, aloes, bamboos, cutch, &c., are exceedingly abundant. Some traces of metals have been found in the southern extremity of the Great Andaman; and in some of the mountain-bases there are beds of bluish schist containing breccia and red ochre. The only quadrupeds are hogs of a small size, and, on the borders of the forests, monkeys, ichneumons, and rats. Birds are scarce and extremely shy. The principal are parroquets, pigeons, crows, king-fishers, curlews, swallows of the species famous for the edible AND nests which they form in the recesses of the rocks, and—as transitory visitors—hawks. The coasts abound with turtle and many varieties of fish, of which the most valuable are mullet, skate, soles, shads, gurnas, pomfrets, rock-fish, sardinas, roe-balls, sable, aloose, cockup, grobers, seer-fish, shrimps, prawns, cray-fish, and a species of whale; they are infested by sharks of an enormous size. Shellfish are abundant; and oysters of excellent quality are met with in some localities. Among the reptile tribes are immense guanoes, snakes, and scorpions. The shores abound with shells of the most beautiful varieties, gorgonias, madrepores, murex, and cowries. The aborigines, whose total number does not exceed 2,500, and who are divided into a number of small hordes, are an anomalous race of the most degraded and repulsive description. They are fierce, cunning, and vindictive, and present in their features and general aspect the appearance of a degenerate class of Negroes. They are perfectly black, and rarely exceed five feet in height. Their heads are large, their shoulders high, their bodies protuberant, and their limbs disproportionately small. The only covering of their persons consists of mud, in which they keep themselves constantly encased as a protection from the noxious insects with which the islands are infested. Their woolly hair they dye with a solution of red-ochre. Their habitations are of the rudest description, consisting of four poles united conically at the top and covered with branches of trees, with an opening only large enough for ingress and egress, and a heap of leaves for a couch. They have no species of vessel capable of resisting the action of fire. The few implements they possess are of the rudest kind. Their chief weapons of offence are bows and lances. The former are usually from 4 to 5 ft. long; the string consisting of a peculiar vegetable fibre, or of a slip of bamboo; and the arrow of reed, pointed with fish-bone, or hard dried wood. Their lances are of iron-wood. They likewise use shields constructed of layers of bark. They employ the bow and spear with great dexterity in the taking of fish, aided by a small hand-net which they make of the filaments of bark. In tempestuous weather these wretched beings are reduced to the most urgent want, feeding on rats, lizards, and snakes; and when such resources fail, often perishing from famine and cold. No sufficient evidence has hitherto been obtained to verify the suspicion of their being anthropophagi. Their canoes are constructed of bamboos, or formed of a piece of timber which they shape by burning or by means of sharp stones. They worship the celestial bodies and various imaginary terrestrial genii, and believe in the existence of an evil genius whom they denominate the demon of storms, and whose wrath they deprecate in a wild species of chorus. The language of the Andamaners seems to present no affinity to that of any other people of the E; but it is affirmed to be not disagreeable, and even their songs are said not to want either melody or expression. The desiderata of the possession of a commodious place of shelter to the E of Bengal for ships of war, and of a depot for the reception of convicts, induced an attempt by the government of Bengal, in 1791, to establish a colony in Port Chatham, a well-sheltered and extremely picturesque indentation at the SE extremity of the Great Andaman. Two years subsequently the attempt was transferred to Port Cornwallis, a bay towards the NE point of the same island, presenting local advantages not inferior to those of the former. But the extreme unhealthiness of the climate, in conjunction with the ferocious dispositions of the natives and their excessive hostility to foreigners, rendered the attempt abortive.
ANDANCE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ardèche, cant. of Serrieres, on the r. bank of the Rhone, 14 m. above Tournon. Pop. 1,381.
ANDAPURGHUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, on the r. bank of the Salundy, 54 m. W of Balasore, in N lat. 21° 25'.
ANDARAVIA, or HINDERABIA, an island in the Persian gulf, in N lat. 26° 45'. It is within a mile of the main, and possesses excellent water.
ANDARD, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Angers. Its principal production is white wine. Pop. 1,123.

ANDAYA, or Andaja, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas Geraes. It takes its rise in the Sierra Matta Gorda, runs in a NE direction, and after a course of 150 m. unites with the San Francisco, about 7 m. above the town of Espiritu Santo. Its gravel is said to contain precious stones, especially diamonds. . ANDAYE. See HENDAYE.

ANDECHS, or ANDEX, a hamlet in the circle of Upper Bavaria, and bailiwick of Starnberg, on an isolated mountain named Heiliger-Berg near Lake Ammer. It contains a church, in which there are several curious tombs, and a castle, formerly a Benedictine monastery.

ANDEER, a parish and v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, pleasantly situated on the r. bank of the Hinter-Rhein, on the Splugen road, 18 m. SSW of Coire, at an elevation of 3,375 ft. above sea-level Pop. 549. It has a considerable cattle-market. In the environs are the mineral springs and baths of Pigneu, and the ruins of the ancient fort of Bärenburg.

ANDEGAST, or Antogast, a hamlet of Baden, in the circle of Kinzig, and bailiwick of Aberkirck, on a lake of the same name, 2 m. SE of Oppenau. Its chalybeate spring has rendered it a place of some celebrity.
ANDELAIN, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Aisne, and arrond. of Laon, 1 m. S of La Fere. Pop. 184. It has manufactures of the sulphate of iron, alumina, and copperas.

ANDELFINGEN, a parish and market-town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Zurich, and the chief place of the district of the same name. It is on the I. bank of the Thur, which is here crossed by a covered bridge, 5 m. above its junction with the Rhine, and on the high road between Schaffhausen and Winterthur, about 7 m. to the SSE of the former town, and 17 m. NNE of Zurich. It contains about 349 houses, and a pop. of 2,566. The surrounding district produces fruits in great luxuriance. Its wines, especially those of the Val de Flaach, are highly esteemed. The vicinity of A. was the scene, in 1799, of several engagements between the Austrians, Russians, and French.

ANDELLE, a small river of France, which rises in the dep. of the Lower Seine, about 3 m. to the SW of the town of Forges, and, running S, enters the dep. of Eure, passes the towns of Charleval and Pont- St.-Pierre, and unites with the Seine about 3 m. above Pont-de-l'Arche, after a course of 33 m., of which 27 are navigable. Its principal affluent is the Lieure. The commerce on this river consists chiefly in the transit of the timber of the forests of Bray and Lions. Near its mouth are the great copper-works of Romilly.

ANDELOT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Upper Marne, on the r. bank of the Rognon, 12 m. NE of Chaumont. It is of great antiquity. Its principal manufacture is cutlery. Pop. of the com. 1,029; of the cant., comprising 19 com. in 1831, 6,219.

ANDELYS (LES), an arrond., canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Eure. The arrond., which contains an area of 100,365 hectares, comprises 6 cant., viz.: Lions-la-Forêt, Econis, Etrepagny, Gisors, Les Andelys, and Ecos; and in 1836 had a pop. of 64,385. The cant., comprising 19 com., had in 1831 a pop. of 12,093. The united townships of Les Grandes and Les Petit Andelys, are situated on the r. bank of the Seine—the former at the distance of 1½ m. from the river in a NE direction—21 m. SE of Rouen, and about the same distance NE of Evreux. Les A. was formerly walled and fortified; and near the Seine is the celebrated fortress of Chateau Gaillard, built by Richard Cour-de-Lion. It had a pop. in 1836 of 5,085. The chief manufactures are fine cloths, cashmeres, ratteens, which equal in quality those of Holland, and silk gauzes. There are also several tanneries, a spinning mill, and 2 printing establishments. In the environs, apples are extensively cultivated for cider. The commerce of A. consists in cattle, grain, wool, linen fabrics, and the scales of the ablet which are used in the manufacture of false pearls. Fairs are held in April, June, and September, lasting each time two days. Antoine de Bourbon, father of Henry IV., died in this town of the wound he received at the siege of Rouen in 1562. In the vicinity is the hamlet of Villers, in which the celebrated Nicolas Poussin was born.

ANDEN, or Andennes, a commune and town of Belgium, the chief place of a cant., in the prov. and arrond. of Namur, on the l. bank of the Meuse, 10 m. ENE of Namur, on the road to Huy. Pop. 5,143. It contains an ancient nunnery with a beautiful church, | AND possesses 2 porcelain manufactories which produce annually 350,000 pieces, 8 manufactories of delftware, 8 of pipes, 4 tile-works, a blast furnace, a paperwork, a cotton-printing work, and several tanneries. The environs abound with clay of various descriptions, and coal. The town of A .-- which is pleasantly situated in a kind of semicircular amphitheatre-was devastated by the Liegeois in 1151; and was burnt in 1467 by the garrison of Huy, during the revolt of Liege against Charles, duke of Burgundy.
ANDEOL-DE-BOURLENC (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, and arrond. of Privas, near the r. bank of the Rhone, 8 m. S of Viviers. Pop. 1,514.
ANDEOL-DE-FOURCHADES (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of Le Chaylard. Pop. 1,086.

ANDERAB, or Inderab, a town of Independent Tartary, situated on a small river of the same name —a tributary of the Oxus—in Kunduz, 160 m. ESE of Balkh, and 90 m. NNE of Cabul, at the opening of the only practicable pass of the Hindoo-Coosh between these two towns. It carries on a considerable transit-trade with Hindostan and Bukharia. The environs afford large quantities of lapis lazuli.
ANDERBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 6 m. E by N of Alford. Area 1,080 acres. Pop. 243.

ANDERLECHT, a commune and town of Belgium, in South Brabant, 2 m. to the W of Brussels, of which it is considered a suburb. The industry of the town consists principally in the manufacture and printing of cotton-fabrics. There are also oil and tan works, gin-distilleries, and iron-founderies. The butter of the environs is much esteemed. Pop. 2,000.

ANDERLUES, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, on the road from Charleroi to Mons, 8 m. to the west of the former town. It has a fine castle, and formerly possessed extensive nailworks. Its principal manufacture is the preparation of chicory-coffee. Pop. 2,662.

ANDERMATT, or Ursern, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Uri, near the Reuss, in a plain 5,722 ft. above the level of the Mediterranean, 41 m. to the N of the St. Gothard, and 18 m. S of Aldorf. It contains a handsome church, two chapels, a capuchin hospital, and a town-house. The principal occupations of the inhabitants are the rearing of cattle and the transport of merchandise. A little to the N of this town are the celebrated Urner Loch or Tron d'Uri, and the Pont du Diable.
ANDERMATTEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, on a small river which flows into the Simmen on the r. bank, 27 m. S of Berne.

ANDERNACH, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, gov. of Coblentz and circle of Mayen, on the l. bank of the Rhine, 9 m. NW of Coblentz, in N lat. 50° 28′, and E long. 7° 23′. This town, once a Roman citadel, retains some interesting relics of its early history; and the massive ramparts and vaulted portals give A. an air of sombre antiquity. A. possesses manufactures of leather, pipes, delft-ware, pottery, and cutlery; and has a considerable commerce in wine, charcoal, potash, mill-stones, and tarras, a species of volcanic sand much used by the Dutch in the construction of their dikes, which is quarried at Brohl and at Kruft. The mill-stones, for which this town has been famous since Roman times, are made of a species of porous lava which is quarried at Lower Mendig near the lake of Laach. A. has the privilege of levying toll on the navigation of the Rhine. In the vicinity of this town, at the village of Namedy, is the great timber-entrepot at which the produce of the forests of the South is collected and formed into floats, usually from 600 to 800 ft. in length and from 60 to 70 ft. in breadth, for conveyance to the port of Dordrecht. The vineyards of the environs are celebrated for the excellence of their produce. This town formerly belonged to the Elector of Cologne.
ANDERNOS, a commune and fishing-port of France, in the dep. of Gironde, arrond. of Bordeaux, on the NE side of the basin or lagune of Arcachon, 25 m. WSW of Bordeaux. Pop. 1,169.

ANDERONIA, a town on the island of Egripos or Negropont, situated near the SW extremity on the 1. bank of a small river, about 1 m. from the coast, 9 m. NW of Karysto, and at nearly the same distance to the WNW of Mount St. Elias.
ANDERSFIELD, a hundred in the county of Somerset, comprising six parishes. Area 10,950 acres. Houses in 1841, 582. Pop. 2,492.
ANDERSKOW, or ANTVORSKOV, a town of Denmark, in Sieland, in the prov. of Soroe, on the road from Slagelse to Naestvid.
ANDERSON, or ANDERSTONE, a parish in Dorsetshire, 7 m. S by E of Blandford-Forum. Area 750 acres. Pop. 43.

ANDERSON, a town of Ohio, U. S., on the Ohio river. Pop. 2,311. It embraces the village of Newton, 10 m. NE of Cincinnati, and all the Virginia military lands which lie in Hamilton co.-Also a central co. of Kentucky, U.S., comprising an area of 170 sq. m., intersected by the head-waters of the Salt river. Pop. in 1840, 18,493, of whom 1,059 were slaves. Its capital is Lawrenceberg.—Also a central co. of E Tennessee, U.S. Its surface, which presents an area of 750 sq. m., is hilly and generally fertile. It is watered by the Clinch river. Pop. in 1840, 5,658, of whom 425 were slaves. Its cap. is Clinton.— Also a district in the NW of S Carolina, U. S., lying between the Saluda and Savannah, and intersected by several of the tributaries of these rivers. It contains about 800 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 18,493, of whom 5,683 were slaves. Its cap., an incorporated v. of the same name, is 127 m. NW of Columbia. Pop. 500.—Also a town of Iowa, U.S., in Bush co. Pop. 1,423.
ANDERSON (CAPE), the eastern extremity of St. Lawrence or Clerk's island, at the southern entrance of Behring's straits.
ANDERSON'S CREEK, a small river of Indiana, U. S., which falls into the Ohio, about 7 m. above the junction of Green river.
ANDERSONVILLE, a post village of South Carolina, U. S., on the Savannah, 145 m. NW of Columbia.
ANDERVILLE (CAPE), a headland of France, near the NE extremity of the dep. of La Manche, 15 m. WNW of Cherbourg.

THE ANDES. An immense chain of mountains, extending in an unbroken chain, nearly parallel with the western coast of South America, from Cape Froward in the straits of Magalhaen, to the mouth of the Atrato in the isthmus of Darien; that is, from S lat. 53° 54', to N lat. 9° 25', or more than 63 degrees, or 4,350 m. in a direct line. The Peruvian name of the chain is Antis, the name of a tribe which dwelt in the province of Quito; or according to others, the native term for copper, from the abundance of which this chain is supposed by them to derive its name. Be this as it may, this name, originally applied by the Incas to a small portion of the chain, is now given to the whole range, and even to its projecting branches; though, strictly speaking, the designation Andes, or Cordillera de los Andes, is confined to that portion of the chain which lies to the S of the equator, for to the N of that line the designation is unknown in the different regions intersected by this great mountain-system. The appellation Andes, however, is even frequently applied to the great continuous range which runs NW through North America. It is conceived that the Mexican Alps, and the Rocky mountains, are a prolongation of the Andes, which are regarded by most geographers as extending from Cape Horn, in S lat. 55° 58', to the mouth of the Mackenzie river, in N lat. 69° 12'. It may be here remarked, that though the chain continues uninterruptedly to the sources of the Cauca and Magdalena, it does not run in a direct meridional line; for Cape Horn, its supposed southern termination, is nearly 27 degrees to the W of Quito, under the equator; the direction, therefore, of the chain is from NE to SW generally; but the inclination is by no means uniform, varying in different parts of its course towards the equator; and thus the sinuosity of the course adds to the length of the chain. At Cape Froward, the chain is narrow, and runs directly N to 21° S lat., where its breadth expands, and the direction trends NNW to 5° S lat., when it turns NNE. At 8° N lat. the chain having completely lost its colossal height, turns westward towards the isthmus of Darien, where it wholly disappears for a considerable distance, and is considered by some geographers to terminate. Throughout their whole extent on the S American continent, the distance of the A. from the ocean is seldom more than 60 m. Their greatest breadth is between the parallels of 15° and 18° S, where a line drawn from E to W, at right angles to the axis of the chain, would measure 400 m. Their mean height, under the equator, is about 15,360 ft.; but this varies exceedingly in different parts of the range. This great chain may be considered as divided into five parts, bearing the names of the regions which it traverses. In Patagonia, from Cape Froward to 41° S lat., it is denominated La Sierra Nevada de los Andes, or 'the Snowy mountains of the Andes.' It bears the same name in Chili; but the common appellation is that of the Andes of Chili. In its passage through Bolivia and Peru, it is known as the Cordillera Real or Royal cordillera, or the Great cordillera of Bolivia and Peru. From the northern frontier of Peru to 2° N lat., its general name is the Chain of Quito. In New Granada it has no general designation; and we may therefore denominate it the Andes of New Granada. The word cordillera is sometimes used alone to designate the A., which are called the Cordilleras of South America. Dr. Pæppig has described the general impression produced by the scenery of the A., as contrasted with that of other great mountain-chains, in the following striking terms: "The character of the A. is widely different from that which everybody, on even a short acquaintance, acknowledges to be the distinguishing one of the Alps of Switzerland and the Tyrol. Without entering into the particulars of natural history, which might detract from the expression of the general features, it is perhaps not quite impossible to give a brief account of this difference. An awful extent of desert, - the perfect nakedness of the immense rocky precipices, a gigantic scale which cannot for a moment be lost sight of,—the scanty vegetation of the gorge-like vallies,-the ceaseless ruin and rolling down of the rocky mountain-walls which extend in endless uniformity and bareness, and a universal savageness that makes one shudder, - such are the principal characteristics of the A. In the outline of the Alps there reigns an extraordinary variety; one peak rises above another; near the form of the rounded dome springs up that of the pointed pyramid, or of the zigzag and grotesquely notched ridge. Not so in the A., which, far and near, appear like an undivided wall with few and widely separated culminating points. Every single group of them presents an immeasurable and uniform mass which fills the mind with the idea of | adamantine fixedness and solidity. But the very circumstance that nature here disdains to heighten the expression of the great by contrast, is the cause why the A. make a much profounder impression on the mind than the Alps: yet, this impression being derived from a single element-vastness, the fancy finds it difficult to retain, for a length of time, a faithful image of such scenes." Magalhaen's Andes.] The great archipelago which lies to the S of the southern extremity of the American continent may be regarded as formed by the crests of a submarine continuation of the A. These islands often rear their summits considerably above the line of perpetual snow, which in so high a latitude is about 3,500 or 4,000 ft. above sea-level. The greatest height in the Tierra del Fuego islands is Sarmiento, in S lat. 54° 27', E long. 70° 51', which has an alt. of 6,910 ft. It rises from a broad base, and has a bi-forked sum- Sarmiento called it Volcan Nevado or 'the mit. Snowy volcano.' Its summit is almost always enveloped in condensed vapour; but when the sky is cloudless, it presents a magnificent appearance. Mount Buckland, in N lat. 54° 26', E long. 7° 22', has an alt. of 4,060 ft. It is a pyramidal block of slate with a sharp-pointed apex. Mount Skyring, in S lat. 54° 25', and E long. 72° 8', has a peaked summit of 3,000 ft. alt.
