Bell Mount, near Strait Le Maire, on the outer coast, has an alt. of 4,000 ft.; while that of Cape Horn is about 3,000 ft. [King.] Patagonian Andes.] The Patagonian division of the A., extending from the frith of Sinsalid to the parallel of 42°, is little known, having been seldom visited. Its height appears to range from 3,000 to 8,000 ft. Its base is here thickly wooded; while from its highest summits glaciers descend to the coast. Melimoyu here attains an alt. of 7,400 ft.; and Yanteles, in S lat. 43° 30', reaches 8,030 ft. The range appears to have at some former period occupied a greater breadth in this portion of the continent, but by some extraordinary convulsion to have had the whole of its western declivity suddenly broken off and submerged in the ocean, in which it forms numerous rocky islets, running parallel to that portion of the chain which has survived the great cataclysm. Several volcanoes exist in this portion of the chain. The sources of the Rio Camarones, which, with the Gallegos, is the only river of note which originates on the eastern flank of the chain, are in the vicinity of the Minchinmadom, which, under the parallel of 42° 48', rises to an alt. of 8,000 ft. To the south of the parallel of 41° 30', the chain approaches so near the sea that the steep islets of the Guaytecas may be regarded as detached fragments from the Andes. The snowy cone of Cuptona is 9,612 ft. high. Chilian Andes.] The Chilian A., extending from the gulf of Ancud, in 42° S lat., to the mountainknot of Porco, in 20° S lat., are elevated in all their points above the limits of eternal snow. Among their summits are Antuco, alt. 13,000 ft., and Aconcagua, alt. 23,200 ft. Their mean breadth is about 120 m. They are steep to the W; and their general distance from the sea is from 85 to 110 m. Between the mountains and the shore are extensive plains from 1,200 to 2,000 ft. above the sea; and on these, as a base, the mountains rise with a steep acclivity to a mean elevation of 12,000 ft. In the northern part of the chain, beyond the high peak of Tupungato, in S lat. 33°, the mountains descend to the coast in a series of terraces, and form a shore bank from 300 to 500 ft. in height. On their western slope upwards of one hundred streams originate; but their courses are far from equalling those of the Rio Negro, the Rio Colorado, and others which descend from the eastern slope towards the Atlantic, watering immense and fertile vallies. The A. of Chili are said to contain fourteen volcanoes, in a state of constant eruption; and a greater number whose eruptions are made at intervals. Earthquakes are frequent here, and often destructive. Nine passes traverse this part of the chain, which, for six months in the year, are nearly impracticable from the snow which then blocks up all communication. The most important of these passes is that by which the great line of road between Buenos Ayres and Valparaiso is carried across the A. Ascending from the western skirt of the Pampas to the city of Mendoza, which has an elevation of 4,486 ft. above sea-level, it passes the Paramillo range, and ascends the Rio de Mendoza to La Cumbre, i. e. 'the Summit,' between Tapungato and Aconcagua, where it has an elevation of 12,700 ft. It then descends the valley of the Quillota to the town of Santa Rosa. The view from the Cumbra is not such as one might expect from the elevation of the ground. The slope of the ridge is gradual on both sides, particularly the eastern; and inferior ridges intercept, in a great degree, the view of the distant plains, which, where partially discovered, seem to fade away in the horizon. The line, apparently strictly horizontal, which marks the limit of the everlasting snows on the surrounding summits of the Cordillera, adds to the stern effect of those perpendicular rocky buttresses which characterise the A. Cordillera of Bolivia and Peru.] From 20° S, near Potosi, where the cordillera of Peru commences, the main range separates into two great branches, running to 14° S, and enclosing between them an extensive and very elevated plateau, extending from Carangas to Lampa, or from the 10th to the 15th parallel, and embracing the small Alpine lake of Paria, the river Desaguadero, and the great Titicaca lake, which, at an alt. exceeding that of the loftiest of the Pyrenees, presents a surface above twenty times the extent of the lake of Geneva, and discharges its overplus waters by evaporation only. The western branch separates this plateau from the coast; the eastern separates it from the immense steppes of Chiquitos and Moxos; and in S lat. 17° 23' it forms a knot sending off towards the E, and to the N of Cochabamba, a transverse snowy range, which rises above the line of perpetual snow, but lowers in its progress till it is lost in the upland plains of the Chiquitos. This transverse branch is denominated the cordillera of Cochabamba, and separates the valley of Gupaiel-Grande from the basins of the Mamore and the Rio Beni. It is also called the Altas de Intinuyo, and the Sierras Altissimas. The western cordillera, in the volcanic peak of Gualatieri, probably attains an elevation of 22,000 ft.; and in the nevado of Anaclache, 18,500 ft. Three enormous nevados or snowy peaks soar above the city of Arequipa; and about 20 m. to the N of that city, Chuquibamba rears his huge trachytic dome to an alt. of 21,000 ft. The eastern side of this cordillera gradually sinks into a valley, itself elevated 13,000 ft. above sea-level. The Bolivian or eastern cordillera has a mean elevation of 16,000 ft. This chain, sometimes known as the Cordillera Real, separates the plateau abovementioned from the immense plain of Chiquitos and the Moxos; and the courses of the Rio Beni, the Madeira, and the Pilcomayo, from those which flow into the Desagnadero, and the lake of Titicaca. From the 14th to the 17th parallel of latitude, the chain attains uninterruptedly the inferior limits of eternal snow; many of its peaks surpass 20,000 ft. in height; and it contains the most elevated summits of the A. which have yet been measured. Those of Sorata, and Illimani, covered with ever-during snow, greatly surpass the gigantic peaks of Colombia, of Chimborazo, Cayambe, Antisana, and Cotopaxi. The Sorata rises in the northern part of the chain, termined trigonometrically at 12,760 ft.; and from other determinations made, at a less distance, of that portion of the mountain which rises above the line of perpetual snow, its total height was found to be 25,400 feet. The nevado of Illimani, only second to that of Sorata, is situated like the former in the Bolivian province of La Paz, 20 leagues ESE of the town of that name. Like Chimborazo in the A. of Quito, it is the most southern snowy summit of the branch to which it belongs. By astronomical observation, it is fixed between 16° 35' and 16° 39' S lat., and 67° and 68° W long. It has four peaks arranged pretty nearly from N to S. Mr. Pentland succeeded in measuring only the most northerly of these peaks, and found its elevation to be 24,000 ft. above the level of the sea, or 12,000 ft. above that of La Paz; but one of the more southern peaks appeared more elevated by 250 ft. The determination of the height of Illimani was made trigonometrically from the borders of a small lake at its base, which was found barometrically to be 15,951 ft. above sea-level. Between the parallel of the Illimani and that of 21° S lat., the eastern cordilleras do not present a single summit which enters the limits of perpetual snow; though several rise to 16,000 feet, and even higher. The Cerro de Potosi, which belongs to it, has an alt. of 16,080 ft. In 21° 15' is the Nevado de Chosolque, 12 leagues NW of Tupisa, the southern frontier of the Bolivian republic. Mr. Pentland met with several peaks covered with eternal snow to the S of this. Between the 13th and 17th parallels Mr. Pentland says the inferior line of perpetual snow is seldom less than 17,000 ft. on the flanks of the eastern chain of the A., or 1,253 ft. higher than the presidency of Quito,—a result, as Humboldt remarks, probably owing, as in Central Asia, to the radiation of caloric arising from the great upland of Titicaca. From S lat. 14° the Andes run NW to S lat. 11°, where they divide into three irregular chains which sink towards the right bank of the Apurimac, named the Cordillera of the Coast, the Central, and the Eastern cordillera. Neither the eastern nor the middle chain attains the elevation of perpetual snow; and the central chain does not reach 10,000 ft. Andes of Quito.] The Andes of Quito commence at S lat. 6° 30', and form only one ridge to 3° 30' where they separate themselves into two distinct ridges, enclosing the valley of Cuença, but uniting again under the parallel of 2° 27', in the mountainknot of Assuay, and again diverging into two chains between 2° 30' and 0° 40' S. In this part of the Andes, the principal summits are ranged in two files, and Spanish academicians, for measuring a degree of the equator, were conducted in the valley between these chains, which has, for that reason, been more exactly and fully described than the other divisions of the Andes. The plain between the higher summits of the two ridges is from 5 to 6 leagues in breadth; and it is within these narrow limits that the whole population of the country is concentrated. "Those who have lived in this elevated spot," says Humboldt, "where the barometer remains at 20 inches, gradually forget that every surrounding object,—the villages full of industrious inhabitants, the pastures covered with herds of lamas and European sheep,the rich cultivated fields waving with the most luxuriant harvests,-hangs, as it were, suspended in the lofty regions of the atmosphere, at more than 9,000 ft. above the level of the sea." The platform of Tapia, where Humboldt took the base of Chimborazo, is elevated 2,891 metres, or 9,476 ft. absolute height; Feet. and at the distance of 183 m. from the mountain. of Tapia, the summit of Chimborazo presented an I angle of only 6.40 above the horizon. Above the plain of Sisgun-which is much nearer than that of Tapia—Chimborazo is elevated only 8,646 ft. About a fourth of its height is covered with perennial snow, and the base of this snowy zone is more than 22,000 ft. round, whilst the dome or cupola, 978 ft. below the summit, is still 4,380 ft. in diam. From its extreme altitude, and the dazzling whiteness of its snows, it is seen at a great distance. It is distinctly visible from the rock of Muerto, to the SW of the Isle of Puna,—a distance of 47 leagues; nay it is visible at a distance of 180 m. at sea, but merely, however, as a single point, the rest of the Andes being invisible. Andes of New Granada. The Andes of New Granada divide themselves into three parallel chains, from the 2d to the 5th parallel of N lat. The eastern chain—which in no part attains the inferior limit of perpetual snow-runs from 2° N lat. at first NNE, under the names of the Sierra of Pardaos and the Paramo of Albarracin, to 6° N lat. where it is called Lomas del Viento. From that point it continues N, and takes the name of the Sierra of Perija, and then that of the Sierra of Azeyte, under which it terminates on the Atlantic, in Point Gallinas. Farther N, under 7° N lat., it sends off, in the Caraccas, a branch which traverses the province of Maracaybo, from SW to NE, under the names of the Sierra of Merida and the Paramo de las Rosas, and goes to join the last branches of the chain of the eastern mountains of South America where they meet the Great cordillera of the Andes. The Sierra of Santa Martha is almost insulated, and separated from the Andes. It is connected by some low hills with the Sierra of Perija, and with the mountains of Ocana and Montuosa. From its eastern side the Rio Negro, the Guaviare, the Meta, and the Apure run, the first to the Maranon, the others to the Orinoco. The Paramo of Porqueros unites the Sierra of Merida to the eastern chain of the Andes, which separates the courses of the Magdalena and Meta, and which is formed by the Paramos of Summa Paz and Thingasa. The central range, which runs N, is situated between the vallies of the Magdalena and the Cauca, and formed by the Nevados of Quindiu, d'Erviz, and Ruis, is generally known as the Quindiu chain. This intermediate chain, illuminated by the rays of the rising and setting sun, presents a most magnificent spectacle when seen from Bogota. It furnishes some of the affluents of the Magdalena and the Cauca, which separates it from the western chain, or that of Choco, and the mountains of Avidi. This last chain is not above 5,760 ft. above the sea at its greatest elevation. It is bounded on the W by the Pacific, and follows the same direction as the central range, first under the name of the Sierra of Sindagua, and then as the Sierra of Veneta. From it, in N lat. 6°, a branch is sent-off towards the isthmus of Panama, but it seems to disappear in its progress long before we arrive at the isthmus. Notwithstanding this interruption, however, as stated in our general article America, the system of the A. is generally considered to be prolonged through the central portion of the continent, to its extreme northern boundary on the Arctic ocean. After traversing the isthmus, with more or less interruption, it separates into two branches on entering the northern continent, which, diverging towards the E and W, but still preserving their direction towards the N, leave in the centre an immense platform or table-land, intersected by the higher points and ridges of the great mountain-chain by which it is supported, but raised in the more central parts to the enormous height of 7,000 ft. above sea-level. This elevation it loses on its approach to | which is the most elevated." It appears, from the the N by the gradual disappearance of the eastern branch, which sinks nearly to a level with the ocean about the 26th parallel, as if to make way for that mighty river by which Texas, Louisiana, and the Floridas are watered; but to the W the cordillera continues in an almost uninterrupted line to the frontiers of the United States, where it splits into various ramifications. [Ward.] General altitude.] The cordillera of the Andes does not present, like the Alps of Switzerland, and the Indo-Tibetian Himalaya, a continuous chain of snowy summits, its elevation being far more unequal than is commonly believed. In some parts of the southern division, between Chimborazo and Loxa, the range is not above 3,200 ft. above the sea. To the N of Quito are several groups of great height, as the Los Pastos in 0° 50′, the volcanoes of Popayan in 2° 25′, the passage of Quindiu in 4° 35', the Sierra of Merida in 7° 58', and that of Santa Martha in 10° 55'. Chimborazo, like Mount Blanc in Savoy, terminates a gigantic group. From this point, to 6° S, no summit appears to reach the limit of perpetual snow, the crest of the Andes not exceeding from 10,240 to 11,520 ft. At 7° S in the province of Guamachuco, the snowy summits become more frequent, and extending themselves uninterruptedly for 10 degrees south, or to 17° S lat. terminate in the Illimani. Under 7° 50', the Andes again raise themselves to the limit of perpetual snow, in the knots of Pasco and Huanuco, in that of Cusco in 13° 50', and in that of Porco in 18° 45'. It is between the parallels of 14° and 17° that the A. attain their maximum of elevation, far exceeding that of Quito, so often described as unequalled on the surface of the globe. We must pile the Rigi, or Mount Athos, on the Chimborazo, to have an image of the highest summit of the Himalaya, the Dhavalagiri [Humboldt]. But in the eastern branch of the Bolivian Andes, the two summits of Sorata and Illimani nearly rival the highest peaks of the stupendous Himalaya; and many of the summits of this eastern branch exceed 20,000 ft. in elevation. In the western branch, though of inferior elevation, we have at least three colossal summits,—Chuquibamba, Gualatieri, and Sahuma,—each 22,000 ft. high, thus surpassing Chimborazo. Several others in this branch are upwards of 20,000 ft. Miste, as measured by Haenk, is 20,376 ft., or according to Curson, who ascended it in 1811, in the month of October, 23,846 ft. Others, as Pichu, and Omati, the volcano of Arequipa, are respectively 18,597, and 18,572 ft. above the level of the sea; and Tacora, or Chipicani, an extinguished volcano, is 18,925 ft. South of Illimani, in 17° S lat., to 21° N lat., the Andes offer not a single summit attaining the limit of constant snow, though several rise to 16,000 and 17,000 ft., as the Inchocajo and Potosi, which have 17,186, and 16,080 ft. of respective elevation. South of this in 21° 15', occurs the Nevado de Chosolque; and to the S of this are several nevados. The points where the main ridge diverges, or throws-off transverse chains, Humboldt denominates knots; and by these he divides the range into separate portions, for the sake of clearer description, and that only. "For," says he, "I do not intend by these the highest parts of a chain, but the points where the parallel branches meet, or the chains re-unite." We have of these knots the Diamante and the Portillo in Chili, and the knots of Potosi, Cusco, Pasco, Assuay, and Los Pastos. "It is the intimate knowledge of these knots," says Humboldt, "that explains the framework of the Andes. Whilst between them are many links, the highest summits belong sometimes to the one and sometimes to the other of these ranges. Amongst three chains running parallel, it is not always the central one, or that which has most snow, observations of Pentland and Gillan that the eastern ranges are higher than the western ranges. Snow line. The mean height of perpetual snow varies in different parts of the Andes range; and by no means depends solely on the latitude. In the region of Upper Peru, the limit seems to have attained a greatly higher elevation than under the equator in the province of Quito. According to Humboldtwho ascertained the mean elevation of this limit by taking the mean of several observations made on the various peaks which on both sides line the longitudinal vale of Quito-the medium elevation of the base of perpetual snow is 2,470 toises, or 15,824 feet: thus at Pichincha, it is 2,498 t. = 15,814 f.; at Chimborazo, 2,471 t. = 15,830 f.; Corazon, 2,458 t. = 15,750 f.; Antisana, 2,458 t. = 15,750 f.; Cotopaxi, 2,538 t. = 16,267 ft.: mean, 2,470 t. = 15,824 f. The inhabitants, so frequently subjected to earthquakes and volcanic eruptions, know that Cotopaxi and Tunguragua divest themselves entirely of their snows previous to an eruption; and the sides of these immense cones seem to have less thickness than those of other volcanoes still active. The mountaineers of the Andes regard the limit of perpetual snows in any particular region as a phenomenon extremely constant: the oscillations of that limit under the equator do not appear to exceed from 76 to 90 ft.; and more frequently the oscillation is insensible. Sometimes, however, those mountains which hardly enter the inferior limit of constant snow are disrobed of all their snow, as has happened to Pichincha. The paramo of Assuay, though its summit only attains 15,749 feet, is yet from 362 to 384 ft. below the inferior limit of constant snow. Under 2° 17' N lat., this limit, at the mouths of the volcano of Purace, is 2,414 toises = 15,448 feet. The snows of Purace border on those of Malva which form a plain of snow of vast extent. Between 8 and 10° N lat., these snows appear in the Sierra and Nevadas of Merida, and Santa Martha, 15,640 ft. high; and the Pico de Horqueta, south of the latter, is more than 3,000 toises, or 19,224 ft. in height. But in the Andes of Bolivia, the lower limit of perpetual snow is far higher than in those of Quito. Here, according to Pentland, this limit is 260 toises, or 1,664 ft. higher than in Quito under the line. Between 13° and 17° S lat., Pentland seldom found this limit below 17,000 ft.; and the cone of Potosi, though near 20° S lat., and 16,080 ft. high, is still under the snow-line. If the elevation of the plateau enclosed by the two branches of the Andes in this division, and the proportionally higher elevation of the inferior limit of perpetual snow be considered, we may perceive why the summits of the Sorata and Illimani do not seem much higher than that of Chimborazo. The relative elevations of these three to the lake of Titicaca, and the cities of La Paz and Quito, are respectively 12,640, 13,150, and 11,934 ft.: the differences being only 706 ft. for the first, and 1,216 ft. for the second above Chimborazo; whilst those of the relative bases are 3,222, and 2,656 ft. higher than Quito. The snows which fall sporadically under the equator assist us in judging of the relative height of mountains. In Quito, the snow never falls below 11,425, and 11,170 ft. It is here that the paramos commence whose mean temperature is below 9°, and on which the thermometer is frequently below 4°. Almost under the equator, in the inhabited plains of Antisana, which are covered with fine turf composed of aromatic plants, snow sometimes falls at 13,456 ft. of elevation, and remains for five or six months. Under the equator, these snows commence at 1,887 toises, or 11,340 ft. Snow never falls on the plateau of the city of Quito at 9,538 ft.; although the centigrade thermometer frequently falls there to 6°, whilst the mean temperature is 14° 4'. Hail may fall once in five or six years, ANDES. from as low as 500 to 300 toises above the sea; but never in the low plains near it. Meteorology.] The temperature of the Andes under the tropics presents remarkable peculiarities. Villages are found there 1,280 ft. higher than the peak of Teneriffe. Under the equator, the heat, near the surface of the earth, has been found to diminish one degree of Fahrenheit's scale for every 324 ft. of elevation. At 10,000 ft. of elevation, the diminution becomes more rapid, one degree being lost in 287 ft.; and at 20,000 ft. the diminution is one degree for every 218 ft. The electric phenomena are grand and terrible in the Andes, particularly in the plateau of Bolivia, around the lake of Titicaca, and in the vicinity of Potosi. The vallies of Calato and Popayan, N of Quito, are noted for thunder-storms; and in those of the Magdalena, the Cauca, the Rio Negro, and the Cassiquiari, these storms are constantly towards midnight. At 9,600 ft. under the line, they are less frequent, and periodical; but they are attended with much hail. Beyond 11,200 ft., thunderstorms are very rare; the hail falls without lightning; and beyond 12,500 ft. it is frequently mingled with snow. The blue of the celestial vault is much more intense under the tropics than at equal elevations in Europe: our nights approach not the majesty of the nights of these regions. Elevated plains.] The Andes, in different parts, present elevated upland plains, such as those of Bogota, Antisana, Suniguaicu, and Caxamarca. That of Antisana is a square of 12 leagues, surrounding the cone of that mountain, which is covered with perpetual snow, and 13,451 ft. high. At the foot of the cone is a hamlet at an alt. of 13,000 feet above sea-level. The plateau of Los Pastos is of a much greater extent, and runs along the back of the range from the city of Pastos to the paramo of Boliche, where the great volcanoes of Combut and Chiles rear their summits. It preserves, on a surface of more than 640 sq. m., an absolute elevation of 9,612 ft.; and its highest point is 10,099 ft. The breadth of the A. on this plateau is not more than 60 m. from SW to NE. The plateaus of Quito and Cumca are disposed longitudinally; those of Bogota and Caxamarca are circular, and enclose basins. The discoveries of Mr. Pentland disclosed the existence of an upland plateau far more elevated and extensive than any, or all put together, of those mentioned by Humboldt,—the plateau of Bolivia or of Titicaca. This elevated upland is more than 400 m. in length, by about 200 m. in breadth; and contains, in its vast expanse, the famed lake of Titicaca covering a surface of 6,000 sq. m. The level of this plateau is more than 13,000 ft. above the sea; and that of the lake itself is 12,760 ft. Here, between 14° and 18° S lat., the habitations of man rise to 15,500 ft. above the sea, nay even to 15,721 ft. at the sources of the Ancomarca; and small villages and post-houses are found at 14,400 ft., as those of Pati and Apo. Many villages have 14,275 ft. of elevation, as that of Tacora between La Paz and Tacua. The most populous towns, as those of Potosi, Puno, Chucuito, are more than 12,800 ft. in elevation; that of Potosi being 13,350 ft. Land is here cultivated at 14,000 ft. of elevation above sea-level. Rye, potatoes, maize, kidney-beans, and European barley, grow abundantly on the shores and islands of the lake of Titicaca. With the exception of the plateau of Tibet, this plateau is the highest on the globe. Volcanoes. No other mountain-chain contains so many volcanoes as the Andes. They are of three kinds,-slender conical peaks, circular summits, and lengthened summits; and exist in every state of activity. "The elastic subterraneous forces strive for the most part in vain to break through the snowclad domes which cover the ridges of the Cordilleras. But even where these forces have actually opened a permanent channel of communication with the outer air, either through crevices or circular craters, they rarely send forth currents of lava, more often erupting ignited scoriæ, jets of carbonic acid gas and sulphuretted hydrogen, and hot steam." [Humboldt.] The first are volcanoes still burning, as those of Purace, Tunguragua, Sangay, and Cotopaxi; whilst the third are those which have sunk after a long succession of ages and eruptions, presenting ridges bristled with points, needles differently inclined, and broken rocks falling into ruins, as those of Capo-Urcu, Carguairazo, and Pichincha. Pichincha was still smoking when visited by Humboldt. Its diameter was 4,520 ft.; and the cone, covered with ashes, rose to 1,545 ft. above the body of the mountain. The whole western range seems to have been a volcanic range; whilst so volcanic is the eastern division that it may be considered as one immense volcano flaming out at different cones, which have at times made dreadful eruptions, not unfrequently accompanied by earthquakes of the most disastrous nature. The last of these, in 1797, destroyed 40,000 persons out of a comparatively thin population. Even so late as November 1827, the village of Purace, famed for its well-built habitations possessed by industrious Indians devoted to agriculture and gardening, was totally destroyed by a volcanic eruption; and the city of Popayan, in its vicinity, has been menaced with the same fate. In Peru numerous volcanoes exist in the western chain which separates the plateau of Titicaca from the coast of the Pacific. The most celebrated are those of Omati, Chipicani, Miste, and Gualatieri. Vapour and smoke continually issue from Gualatieri, and sometimes flames. The crater of the Miste is immense; its opening being 1,800 ft. from NW to SE, and 900 in breadth. The volcano of Chipicani has an open crater on the eastern part of the summit; and a little to the NW a less elevated upland presents the remains of an extinguished volcano,—a real solfatara, whose vapours are condensed in the Rio Azufrado. In the Chilian A., volcanoes are numerous and active; and of these, the volcano of Pucquines, at the source of the Maypu, has of late years been very active. Many others appear in the Araucanian and Patagonian A.; but of these we have little knowledge. Earthquakes are frequent and destructive on both sides of the Andes, from the straits of Magalhaen, to the upper vallies of the Cauca and Magdalena. Geology. The A. rest on a granitic base, supporting the lofty frame-work like the secondary formations of the plains. The quartzose granite of the A. seems older than the small grained granite which abounds in the small hexagonal tables of mica, sometimes in mass, sometimes divided in regular strata inclined and parallel, encasing the very micaceous rounded masses. The granite of Peru resembles that of the High Alps and Madagascar. Upon this rock, and sometimes alternating with it, gneiss appears, which passes into micaceous schist, and this again into primitive schist. In the A., as in the cordillera of Venezuela, and in that of Paria, the primitive rocks, especially the gneiss and micaceous schist, present an almost constant inclination to the northwest. Granite in these regions is more common in the stratified granite than in the micaceous schist; it appears even in the beautiful porphyry, which, placed on the primitive schist, crowns the summit of the argentiferous mountain of Potosi. Calcareous granated rock, chloritic schist, and primitive trap, frequently form subordinate beds in the stratified granite and micaceous schist. The highest summits of the A. present throughout porphyritic formations, with basalts, pho- ANDES. nolites, and green rocks. These formations, frequently divided into columns, seem as so many grotesque forms of ruined castles when seen at a distance. At the foot of the A. two formations of very distinct brown freestone are found; the one has a siliceous base encasing primitive masses, and sometimes cinnabar and beds of coal; the other has a limestone base cementing the secondary rocks. Plains of more than 60,000 sq. leagues are covered with an old conglomerate containing fossil-wood and brown iron-ore: on this rests primitive limestone, characterised by frequent beds of clay, schist, and small lines of blue calcareous spath. It serves as a base for lamellar gypsum filled with sulphur, and frequently muriatiferous. After the lamellar gypsum, follows another formation of a very homogeneous limestone, whitish and sometimes full of caverns; then a calcareous gris; then a fibrous gypsum without muriate of soda, but mixed with argil; and finally, calcareous masses usually containing flint and hornstone. One of the most frequent phenomena in the Andes is the immense height to which the posterior rocks raise themselves above the granite, and the thickness of the formations. The highest point of granite which Humboldt saw is in the mountains of Quindiu, at 11,500 ft. The frozen summits of Chimborazo, Cayambe, and Antisana, are porphyritic, at an elevation of from 19,224 to 21,472 ft. The secondary calcareous rock is elevated 12,176 ft. at Micuipampa. The brown freestone of Huancavelica appears at 14,804 ft.; micaceous schist appears at Tolima, the summit of the paramo of Gouanacas in New Granada, at 15,250 ft.; the basalt of Pichincha, at 15,568 ft. Beds of coal appear near Santa Fe at 8,660 ft.; and near Huanuco in Peru at 14,740 ft. The plains of Bogota are full of brown freestone, gypsum, and shelly limestone-rock; and near Zypaquera, of rock-salt, at 8,968 ft. The stupendous mountain of Illimani, near La Paz, is composed of secondary rocks, transition slate, and mica slate, closely resembling those of the High Alps of St. Jean de Maurienne, and the Tarantaise in Savoy. These are traversed by numerous quartzose veins containing auriferous pyrites and native gold. The volcanic summits of the western range present a succession of trachytic domes, composed of trachyte and trachytic conglomerates, resting on a high upland of red sandstone: as in the province of Carangas. The debris of organized bodies rarely occurs, because limestone rock is by no means abundant near the equator. Petrified shells, however, have been found near to Micuipampa, in 6° 45', at 12,816 ft.; and near Huancavelica at 14,140 ft. The fossil bones of the carnivorous elephant have been found in the cordillera at from 7,560 to 9,584 ft. of elevation. In the A. of Peru, as well as in Mexico, porphyritic beds occur at an altitude of from 10,252 to 12,816 ft. The porphyries of Chimborazo are 18,000 ft. thick; and a formation of pure quartz, to the W of Caxamarca, is 4,800 ft. thick. None of these formations is interrupted by other heterogeneous formations. Another equally interesting phenomenon of the equatorial regions, is the great abundance of porphyries, always containing amphibole, but never quartz, and rarely mica. The great sulphureous masses so abundant in the Andes, appear far distant from the volcanoes, not in gypsum and limestone, but in the primitive mountains. Botany.] "The different climates of the earth," says Humboldt in his Cosmos, "and the vegetable forms of which they determine the succession, are placed one over another, stage above stage; and the laws of the decrement of heat are indelibly written on the rocky walls and the rapid slopes of the Cordilleras, in characters easily legible to the intelligent observer. Not only is the torrid zone, through the abundance and luxuriance of its forms, most rich in powerful impressions, it has also another advantage in the uniform regularity which characterises the successions both of meteorological and of organic changes." Below the region of perpetual snow, in places where snow falls momentarily, bare rocks appear on the summits of the A., in the midst of sterile deserts, and bordered with different species of lichens which indicate the commencement of vegetation. A species of wiry grass seems to be the usual food of the guanacos and vicunas, and is the distinctive mark of the paramos, where no tree grows, where no rain falls, and but a little snow at times is seen. From 15,120 ft., to the region of perpetual snow, lichens cover the rocks: some even seem to exist under the eternal snows, as Humboldt saw them towards the summit of Chimborazo, at the height of 18,240 ft. This is the very highest point at which vegetation has yet been found in the A.; though it may reach still higher in the A. of Titicaca. From 13,456 to 15,120 ft. is the region of the gramineæ, or grasses. Snow falls occasionally on this region. Below this is the region of Alpine plants, extending from 6,574 to 13,456 ft. of elevation. At the equator, the region of great trees—or such as have trunks from 60 to 90 feet high—is not elevated beyond 8,860 ft. From the level of the valley of Quito, the trees diminish in size, and their height is not comparable to that which the same species reach in more temperate climates. At 11,500 ft., trees cease altogether, and common shrubs commence. The ground is here covered with a multitude of calceolarias, whose corolla, from its golden colour, contrasts most agreeably with the verdure of the carpet on which they rise. Still higher, appear the wintera and escallonia. This region is from 9,200 to 10,848 ft. The cold but always moist climate of these heights called paramos, produces dwarf trees, whose trunk, short and crooked, divides itself into an infinity of branches, covered with rough leaves, and of a shining green. All arborescent plants cease at 11,500 ft. Some shrubs of orangecoloured quinquina, of the embethrium and the melastoma, with yellowish purple flowers, reach this utmost point. At the height of 11,834 ft., cultivation ceases in the Andes of Quito; though it is carried up to more than 14,000 ft. in the plateau of Titicaca. Beyond 10,835 ft., no grain appears. From 9,840 to 13,211 ft., the chief object of culture is the potato. From 6,564 to 9,840 ft. is the region where the cultivation of the various European cerealia is conducted, and of quinoa. This cultivation, however, reaches, on the shores of the lake of Titicaca, an elevation of 12,760 ft., and is even carried higher near La Paz. In Quito, maize is chiefly cultivated beyond the elevation of 7,680 ft.; but the frosts and hail in that province, which occur from 10,256 to 10,880 ft., frequently injure the cultivation of wheat. Beyond 5,760 ft., the banana is reared with difficulty. Between 5,260 and 6,260 ft., is the region, in Quito and New Granada, where wheat is most abundant; and its lowest termination is about 4,500 ft. Sugar is successfully cultivated in Quito, at an elevation of even 8,328 ft. From 3,280 to 6,560 ft. is the most temperate climate for European plants; and from the level of the sea, up to 3,280 ft., the natives cultivate bananas, maize, manioc, and cacao. This also is the region of the anana, the orange, the pine-apple, and the most delicious fruits. Between 5,260 and 6,260 ft. is the region of the cocca (Erythroxylum Peruvianum). The region of the scitamineæ and of palms reaches from the sea to 3,283 ft. It is the abode of the musa, the heliconia, the alpinia, and the most odoriferous liliacea. It is in this burning climate that the balsam of Tolu, and the quinquina of Cacory (Cortex Angosturæ) vegetate. The scitamineæ, and especially the heliconia, cease at 2,624 ft.; and the palms-which here, no more than in the Old world, can support the cold of high mountains-cease at 3,283 ft.: one species only vegetates at a height equal to that of Mount Cenis. The Ceroxylon andicola, or wax-palm, grows in the Andes of Quindiu and Tolima, at from 6,110 to 9,432 ft.; beyond that region rise the tree-ferns and the Peruvian bark or quinquina. The latter has a much greater extent than the former, which love a temperate climate, and are not found beyond 5,260 ft. on the slope of the equatorial Andes: while the quinquina is found as high up as 9,513 ft. The true cinchonas, or quinquinas, appear in the cordillera of the Andes, all the way from the Snowy mountains of Santa Martha to Potosi, near 20° S lat. The eastern side of the Andes, from the south of Huanuco, near the mines of Tipuani, in the vicinity of Apollobamba and Yuracardis, is one continued forest of quinquina. It has been found even near Santa Cruz de la Sierra, to the east of the eastern Andes. The cuspare of Guayana, the cusparia of New Andalusia, and the cascarilla of Allacamez, all vegetate at the level of the sea. Vegetation is less varied in the high plateau of Riobamba and of Quito, as also in that of Pastos as far as Almaguir, than in the other equally elevated regions. The great volcanic catastrophes to which the country is subjected appear to have diminished the species of plants. In the temperate region of the cinchonas, grow also some liliaceas, the melastoma with great violet flowers, the passiflora in trees high as the oaks of the north, the Bocconia frutescens, the fuchsia, and the alstræmeria, of rare beauty. The ground is covered with mosses. The ravines conceal the gunnera, the oxalia, and an unknown multitude of arums. Towards 5,580 ft. of elevation, appear the citrosma with odorous leaves and flowers, and numerous species of the symplocos. Beyond 7,800 ft., the mimosa, whose irritable leaves close at the touch of the hand, no longer appears. Between 8,538 and 9,848 ft. is the region where oaks most abound. These do not commence in the equatorial regions below 5,888 ft.; whereas in Mexico they descend to 2,624 ft. above the sea, and are found at an elevation of 13,123 ft. No firs grow in the A. between the tropics, though they abound in the forests of North America. The secondary mountains of the A., as might be expected, are covered with majestic forests; and it is from their flanks, and the transversal vallies which they form, that the numberless torrents issue whose waters go to swell the immense streams which compose the ocean-flood of that monarch of streams, the Amazon. In the subjoined cut A represents the alt. of the city of Mexico; B, the volcanic summit of Popocatepetl; C, of Cotopaxi; D, Chimborazo; E, the Nevado de Serata; F, the Nevado Illimani; G. Aconcagua; d, the highest point (19,695 ft.) to which Boussingault ascended on Chimborazo; e, the point attained by Bonpland (18,286 ft.); f, the alt. of Ancomarca; g, the highest limit of barley cultivation; H, the city of Potosi; k, the highest limit of maize cultivation; c, the limit of the Pinus occidentalis; b, of the oak; m, of cinchonæ; n, the limit of great trees; o, of tree-ferns; and p, of the palm. Animals.] Animals of all kinds, wild and tame, carnivorous and graminivorous, destructive and useful, abound in the Andes. In the higher regions, of from 13,148 to 16,436 ft. of elevation, are found flocks of vicunas, guanacos, and alpacas. The lama exists only in a domestic state. These animals follow the chain of the A. of Chili and Peru, to 9° S lat. The limit of perpetual snow is the upper limit of organized beings. The condor is the only animal which inhabits the vast solitudes rising above this line. Humboldt has seen it soaring aloft at an elevation equal to that of Chimborazo. Passes.] The vallies of the A., which are greatly more deep and narrow than those of the Alps and Pyrences, present scenes calculated to fill the mind with terror and astonishment. Crevices, or transversal valleys, called quibradas, or breaks, by the Spaniards, everywhere tear, and as it were, furrow the ridge of the A. These are often 2,000 ft. deep, with a stream in the middle; and have their sides and bottoms clothed with exuberant vegetation. At the sight of these deep chasms, the imagination of an European is much more powerfully affected than by that of the majestic summits of Chimborazo, Cayambe, or Cotopaxi, whose effect is weakened by their situation on such lofty bases as those of Tapia and Suniguiacu; while no such diminution is suffered in the quibradas, whose depth, in many places, is so great that were Vesuvius, or the Puy de Dome, or Ben Nevis the loftiest of our Scottish Alps, seated in these abysses, their summits would not rise above the nearest ridges. The valley of Ardissan, which descends from Mount Perdu in the Pyrenees, is 2,950 ft. in depth; but those of Chota and Cutaco were found by Humboldt's measurement to be respectively 4,920 and 4,260 ft. deep: and their bottoms are only one-fourth less elevated than the passes of St. Gothard and of Mount Cenis in the Alps. The humidity of the climate, and the steep declivities in many places, give birth to torrents which descend in channels only a foot or a foot and a half broad, but from 20 to 25 feet deep. The paths formed by these resemble a gallery cut in, with nothing but the sky overhead. With trembling steps and slow, the traveller has oft to trace his way through these crevices, darkened by the thick and rank vegetation which covers them overhead. The pass of Quindiu, in N lat 4° 36', lies through a thick forest, and is, on that account, the most difficult of any in the Andes, and cannot be traversed in less than ten or twelve days even in the best weather. Travellers are always compelled to lay in a month's provision beforehand, as it frequently happens that, from the melting of the snow, and the sudden swell of the torrents, it is impossible to descend in any direction. The summit of this pass-the Garito del Paramo-is 11,499 ft. above the sea, and 8,300 ft. from the base of the mountain. The pathway is only from 12 to 16 inches wide; and in several places seems merely a deep gallery cut in with the sky over-head. Travellers are here carried by men in a chair tied on the back, for it is impossible to traverse the pass of Quindiu on mules. "To talk of going on a man's back," says Humboldt, "is no more than that of going on horseback; no humiliating idea is attached to the profession of a carguero, or man-carrier; and these carriers are not Indians nor negroes, but mulattoes, and even sometimes whites." The passes which surround the prov. of Antioquia, to the N of the Quindiu, are traversed in the same way. In the pass of Quindiu, not a hut is to be seen to lodge either the traveller or his carriers; but the latter provide themselves with several hundred leaves of the banana plant, in the mountains before they take their journey. These leaves, of a membranous and silky form, are oval, and 20 inches long, by 14 broad. When a spot is reached in the midst of the forests where the ground is dry, and where it is resolved to pass the night, the cargueros lop off a few branches from the trees with which they make a tent. In a few minutes this tent is divided into squares by the stalks of some creeper, or the thread of the agave; and having unrolled the banana leaves, these are spread over the whole work so as to cover each other like the tiles of a house. The tents, thus hastily erected, are cool and comfortable; and keep the inmates perfectly dry amidst violent and incessant rains. Bridges.] Most of the torrents which roll down the steeps of the A. are fordable, except when swelled by rain, when their rapidity is such that travellers must wait several days ere they can be crossed. But when they are too deep to be forded, or the banks are inaccessible, bridges of a singular make, like those in the Himalaya, are thrown over them, and though like them of an apparently dangerous and fragile form, they answer the purpose intended admirably well. When the river is narrow, with high banks, these are constructed of wood; and consist only of long beams laid across the precipice, forming a path of a yard and ahalf broad, just sufficient to be crossed by a man on horseback. When the stream is too broad to admit of beams being thrown across, cane-bridges made of the bijuco—a slight and elastic species of cane-are formed of the length required. Six of these are stretched across the stream, two of which are considerably higher than the other four, and the whole being covered with sticks laid transversely, is passed by men, while the mules swim across. These cane-bridges appear like ribands suspended above a crevice or impetuous torrent, and are really terrific to the view. "The danger is not very great," says Humboldt, "when a single person passes across the bridge as quickly as possible, with his body leaning forward. But the oscillations of the rope become very great when the passenger is conducted by an Indian who walks quicker than himself; or when frightened by the view of the water which he sees through the interstices of the bamboos, he has the imprudence to stop in the middle of the bridge, and lay hold of the ropes which serve him as a rail." Another species of bridge is a tarrabida, which consists of a single rope made of bijuco, and extended across the river, on each bank of which it is fastened to strong posts. On one side is a kind of wheel, or winch, to straighten or slacken the rope to the degree required. From this rope hangs a leathern hammock capable of holding a man, to which a rope is fastened to draw it across. For carrying over mules, two ropes are necessary, and they are thicker and stronger. The creature being suspended from these ropes, and secured by girths round the belly, neck, and legs, is shoved off, and immediately landed on the opposite side. These tarrabidas are, like those in the Himalaya, laid across the various torrents which, foaming and roaring in dark and fathomless abysses, pursue their wild and rapid courses to the plains of Hindostan. Dr. Poeppig gives the following account of the manner in which he crossed the river of Aconcagua: "The mode of crossing the river—which is far from inconsiderable—is of a very peculiar kind whenever the height of the flood renders the ordinary method of fording impracticable. On both banks are men whose occupation it is to conduct travellers through the bounding torrent, which in some places breaks violently over concealed rocks. These men are called vaqueanos; they are peasants, take pride and pleasure in their dangerous occupation, and are in general remarkable for their great bodily strength and for their large and well-trained horses. The preparations are soon made: as soon as the party plunge into the stream, the guides press themselves close to the traveller on both sides, and fasten him between them in such a way that, even if he were to lose his balance, he cannot easily fall from the saddle. These men govern the horses with such unerring skill that they remain constantly pressed together. Wherever the depth is so great that the horses must swim, the guides seize the reins of the middle horse, and bid the traveller shut his eyes lest his head grow dizzy. The water rushes by with astounding rapidity and deafening noise; and, in our case, was cold enough to make all three complain of the bath, since even the horse that swims best plunges so deep into the stream as to wet his rider to the hips. It is not until after being freed from the tight grasp of the two guides, that one perceives that they hold the lasso always ready, in order to save the traveller, if, in spite of their precautions, he should be snatched away by the waves. On the coasts of Peru, between Lambayeque and Truxillo, are many broad mouths of rivers, or rather arms of the sea, which can be crossed in this way alone, since, though generally too shallow to admit of boats, they have many deep places through which it is necessary to swim. There the chimbador, who takes the office of the Chilian vaqueano, makes the traveller mount behind him, while he manages the horse. It is said that these men (the chimbadors) who are usually men of colour, and who ride into the water in a state of perfect nakedness, always carry with them a long knife for the purpose of stabbing the passenger, should the latter fall off, and, grasping his guide in the agony of despair, threaten to involve both in a common destruction." Shortly after his visit to Aconcagua, Dr. Poeppig set out on a journey across the cordillera to Mendoza, and bivouacked in a wild ravine near a bounding torrent which I. AND rushed from beneath the perpetual snows of the surrounding heights. Early next morning he proceeded to cross the torrent, not above twenty paces wide. His Chilian servant got over safely; but he himself was carried away by the force of the current, and escaped destruction only by springing on a rock in the middle of the stream, whence he was dragged ashore by means of the lasso. In the meantime he saw two of his laden mules whirled off with the speed of lightning by the angry torrent, and, shooting down the ravine, disappear over a cataract. Authorities.] La Figure de la Terre, par Bouguer. -Humboldt, Esquisse d'un Tableau géognostique de l'Amerique Meridionale. - Proctor's Narrative of a Journey across the Cordillera of the Andes. 1823.-Smith and Lowe's Narrative of a Journey from Lima to Peru. 1836.—Royal Geographical Society's Journal.—Humboldt's Cosmos, 1846.—Darwin's Geological Observations on South America, 1846.
ANDEVILLE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Oise and arrond. of Beauvais, 2 m. NNE of Muré. It has extensive manufactures of toys, trinkets, and fans. Pop. 909.

ANDEVOURANTE, or DEVOURANTE, a large river of Madagascar, which rises in the Ambohistemene or Red mountains, flows to the E through the country of the Betanimenes, and enters the Indian ocean near a village of the same name, which was formerly considered the largest in the island.
ANDGEH-BURUN, or CAPE INDJEH, a headland of Anadolia, on the Black sea; forming the most northerly point of the peninsula; 15 m. NW of Sinope, in N lat. 42° 8', E long. 34° 56'.
